Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
Mary Lennox, a sickly, sour-faced child born in India, is sent to Misselthwaite Manor after a cholera outbreak kills her parents. Her mother, a beautiful socialite, neglected her, leaving Mary to be raised by servants who obeyed her every whim, making her tyrannical and selfish. On a hot morning, Mary wakes to find her Ayah gone. She throws a tantrum, kicking a replacement servant. Later, she overhears her mother talking fearfully with a young officer about the cholera. As wailing erupts from the servants’ quarters, her mother flees inside. During the chaos, Mary hides alone in the nursery, eating leftovers and drinking wine that makes her drowsy. Forgotten by everyone, she falls asleep, isolated and uncared for.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Concrete Details
- **Disagreeable appearance:** She has “a little thin face,” “thin light hair,” and “a sour expression.”
- **Tyrannical and selfish:** She “beat and kicked” a servant who replaced her Ayah because she didn’t get her way.
- **Ill-tempered:** She wakes “feeling very cross” and mutters insults like “Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” at a servant.
- **Fretful and sickly:** She has been “ill in one way or another” since birth and is described as a “sickly, fretful” baby and toddler.
- **Neglected:** Her mother handed her to an Ayah to “keep the child out of sight,” and during the cholera, “nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her.”

### Setting and Emotional Distance/Isolation
The cholera-stricken bungalow in India creates intense emotional distance by physically and socially isolating Mary. She is “forgotten by everyone” as servants flee or die, and her mother ignores her even in crisis. The eerie, empty dining room with a hastily abandoned meal and “mysterious and frightening sounds” from the huts heighten her loneliness. No one explains the chaos, leaving Mary to wander alone, eat unsupervised, and drink wine to cope. The setting’s panic and death cut her off from all human connection, reinforcing her profound neglect.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary reacts by kicking and screaming when the new servant comes instead of asking for help? How would you feel in her place?
2. The story says Mary was “kept out of sight” as a baby. How might being ignored by her parents have affected the way she behaves?
3. During the cholera outbreak, Mary is “forgotten by everyone.” What clues in the passage show that no one is taking care of her?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water on Earth, stored in the atmosphere, on land, and underground. Oceans hold 96% of Earth’s water; the rest is fresh water in lakes, rivers, ice, and groundwater. Water changes form—liquid, solid, or gas—and moves via evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. Solar energy and gravity drive this cycle. Human water use, land use, and climate change affect water movement, storage, and availability. Understanding these impacts supports sustainable water use.

### Concept Map: Water Movement Among Major Pools
```
Atmosphere (water vapor)
      ↑  evaporation/evapotranspiration  ↓  precipitation
      ↓                                 ↑
Oceans & Land Surface (liquid/solid)
      ↓  runoff/streamflow/snowmelt  →  Rivers, Lakes, Reservoirs
      ↓  infiltration
Groundwater (aquifers)
      ↓  natural discharge (springs, rivers, ocean)
      →  back to Oceans & Surface
```

### Key Terms (8+)
- **Water cycle**: The continuous movement of water on Earth among storage pools.
- **Evaporation**: The change of liquid water to water vapor, driven by solar energy.
- **Evapotranspiration**: The combined movement of water into the atmosphere from land and oceans, mainly via plants and evaporation.
- **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail falling from clouds due to gravity.
- **Runoff**: Water moving across the land surface toward streams, rivers, or oceans.
- **Infiltration**: The entry of water into the ground from the surface.
- **Groundwater recharge**: The process by which infiltrated water replenishes underground aquifers.
- **Aquifer**: An underground layer where groundwater flows and is stored.

### Human Influence
Human actions affect the water cycle through water use (e.g., withdrawal for drinking, irrigation, industry), land use (e.g., paving surfaces, deforestation, agriculture), and climate change. These activities alter water movement (e.g., reduced infiltration from paved surfaces), storage (e.g., draining wetlands, building reservoirs), and availability (e.g., depleting groundwater, changing precipitation timing). Understanding these impacts helps guide sustainable water management.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on warehouse system migration and current shipping delays  

**Team,**  

I want to give you a direct, factual update on the warehouse management system migration that went live last Monday, and what it means for our customers, for your teams, and for the next several days.  

**What happened**  

We completed the scheduled migration to a new warehouse management platform on Monday, April 10. This migration was designed to improve long-term picking accuracy, inventory visibility, and shipping speed. However, during the cutover we encountered unexpected data-mapping errors between the new system and our existing order routing logic. The errors caused certain pick waves to be duplicated, others to be dropped, and processing instructions to be sent in the wrong sequence to our packaging stations.  

The result: for the first four days of this week, about 60% of orders that entered our system after the cutover were delayed by 24–48 hours. As of today, the backlog is approximately 8,500 unshipped orders. On a normal week, that number would be under 1,000.  

The root cause was an incompatibility between a legacy field structure in our inventory database and the new system’s order-construction logic. We identified the specific field mismatch on Wednesday afternoon and deployed a fix Thursday morning. No single team or vendor made a negligent decision. The testing environment did not replicate the full data volume we see in production—a gap we are now addressing.  

**Current customer impact**  

- **Shipping delays:** Customers who placed orders between Monday and Wednesday are now receiving estimated delivery dates 3–4 days later than normal. Orders placed Thursday and Friday are still processing, but the majority are clearing within 24 hours again as of this morning.  
- **Support volume:** Inbound contacts are up roughly 3x compared to our average week. The majority of inquiries are “Where is my order?” and “Can I cancel or change my shipping method to expedite?” Our support team is handling about 950 contacts per day, with average wait times holding around 8 minutes—longer than our standard, but not yet critical.  
- **Customer tone:** Many customers are frustrated because they received standard “shipped” notifications for orders that were then delayed in the routing step. They feel misled, which is fair. We are working to send corrected tracking information within 12 hours of the fix being applied.  

**What we are doing today**  

1. **Clearing the backlog** – Overnight, the warehouse team re-processed all 8,500 backlogged orders through a manual routing script. As of 6:00 AM, 6,200 of those are now in carrier possession. The remaining 2,300 are in final labeling and should be out by end of day.  
2. **Preventing reoccurrence** – Engineering deployed a validation check that compares order fields against the new system schema before any pick wave is released. This catch will prevent the same mismatch from happening again.  
3. **Support surge** – We added five temporary agents this morning, and customer service leadership extended the team’s shift by two hours through Monday. Every agent has a direct escalation path to our shipping operations lead (see below).  
4. **Customer communications** – Every delayed customer will receive a personalized email today with their actual tracking number and a sincere explanation of the delay. No generic apologies; each email includes a $10 credit valid on their next purchase.  

**What changes next week**  

- **Monday:** The system will undergo a full load-test at 2x our average daily volume, using synthetic data. If that passes, we will increase order flow to 100% on Tuesday.  
- **Tuesday:** Our support team will begin proactive outbound calls to any customer whose order was delayed more than 72 hours. This is not standard, but it is necessary for this group.  
- **Wednesday:** We will publish a post-mortem internally (and a public version on our status page) that details the technical root cause, the testing gap, and the new protocols.  
- **Ongoing:** Every system migration going forward will include a mandatory 24-hour “shadow run” with live data after cutover, before full volume is released.  

**How to escalate urgent issues**  

If you encounter a customer escalation that involves:  
- An order that remains unshipped after today’s clearance process,  
- A customer who was charged twice or received duplicate items,  
- A safety or compliance concern (e.g., damaged goods, hazardous material handling),  

Please ping **#ops-escalations** in Slack with the order number and a one-sentence description. For any other support or logistics questions, use the normal channels, but tag @ship-ops-lead and @support-lead. They are both monitoring actively.  

**Manager talking points for team huddles**  

If your team wants a concise version for stand-ups or one-on-ones, here are the four key messages:  

1. **Our system migration caused order routing errors last Monday through Thursday. The fix was deployed Thursday morning, and the backlog is being cleared today.**  
2. **Customer support volume is elevated due to shipping delays and incorrect tracking notifications. We added agents, and we are sending corrected tracking and credits to impacted customers.**  
3. **No team or vendor is being blamed. We identified a data-mapping gap in our testing, and we are implementing pre-release validation for all future migrations.**  
4. **Escalate any unshipped orders after end of day today via #ops-escalations. Normal routing resumes Monday after load-testing.**  

**Closing**  

I know this week has been demanding, especially for our warehouse, support, and engineering teams who have worked through late nights and shifting priorities. Thank you for the focus and professionalism.  

We will be transparent about what went wrong, fix it fully, and make sure the next migration does not repeat this pattern. If you have direct feedback about how the situation was handled—or questions about what we could have done differently—my calendar is open Friday afternoon, or you can drop me a Slack.  

Regards,  

[Your Name]  
COO  
[Company Name]

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
After the summer rain, the garden smelled like wet dirt and green. Every leaf shivered with leftover drips, and the flagstone path was a mirror for the sky. That’s when Barnaby the snail began his afternoon work.

Barnaby was a careful snail. He carried a home on his back and a tiny map in his head. The map showed every puddle in the garden: the Deep One by the rosebush, the Shallow Spreader under the hose, and the Tiny Twinklers that hid in the lily pad cups. Barnaby did not splash or rush. He slid slowly from puddle to puddle, checking his map with his squishy tentacles. A puddle that was deep yesterday might be shallow today. A puddle that was wide in the morning might be a dark spot by supper. Barnaby knew that water, like time, moved without asking permission.

“Puddles are not forever,” he often whispered to himself. “That is why I must keep my map.”

As he reached the Big Wobbler—a puddle that looked like a tear on the stone—a shadow fell over him. A young sparrow dropped from the oak branch, landing not gracefully, but with a thump and a tumble.

“Oof!” said the sparrow, scrambling up. Her name was Pip. She had a patch of white feathers on her chest that made her look like she’d spilled milk on herself.

“Hello,” said Barnaby.

“Hello, slow-go!” chirped Pip. She hopped around the puddle. “I need to fly. Right now. Up to the very top of the oak. To the yellow leaf that touches the cloud.”

Barnaby tilted his head. “Why now?”

“Because the rain is over! The sky is scrubbed clean! The wind is calling!” Pip fluttered her wings. They were damp, and she could not lift off. “I can fly UP. I just can’t do the landing part. Landing is boring. Landing is for grown-ups.”

Barnaby looked at his map. The Big Wobbler was still there, but he could see a tiny edge where the water was pulling back, like a blanket shrinking. “Flying is like a puddle,” he said.

Pip stopped hopping. “What does that mean?”

“It means you cannot reach the sky without understanding the ground.”

Pip puffed her chest. “I don’t need the ground. I need the wind.”

Barnaby did not argue. He was too slow to argue. Instead, he pointed his tentacle at a puddle near the compost bin. “See that puddle? It is drying. The sun is drinking it. By late afternoon, it will be gone. If you want to fly to the top of the oak, you will have to come back down. The ground is always waiting.”

Pip did not like that. She hopped away and tried again to lift off. She jumped, flapped hard, and rose three inches—then fell sideways into a muddy patch. Her white chest was now brown. She shook herself, frustrated.

“I told you,” said Barnaby gently.

“I don’t need lessons from a snail who maps puddles,” she muttered.

Barnaby did not get upset. He was too slow for that, too. But he did something unusual. He said, “I will show you something.”

Pip followed him—impatiently, hopping ahead and then waiting, then hopping again. Barnaby slid past the rosebush, past the hose, and stopped at a very ordinary puddle. It was round, shallow, and perfectly still. “This one has no name on my map yet,” he said. “Because I have not watched it long enough.”

Pip looked at the puddle. “It’s just water.”

“It is not just water,” said Barnaby. “Watch.”

He leaned close. A tiny whirl of breeze crossed the puddle, and the surface wrinkled. A seed floated, spinning. And then—slowly—the wind pushed the water to one side, and the puddle changed shape. Not much. Just a little.

“The wind works on puddles,” said Barnaby. “It moves them, dries them, changes them. The wind is powerful. But even the wind cannot make a puddle disappear instantly. It takes patience.”

Pip sat down. Her wings drooped. “I want to fly so badly.”

“I know,” said Barnaby. “And you will. But first, learn to land. Watch the puddle. See how the water always returns to the lowest place? That is how the ground catches you. It is the bravest thing the water does—to be still when it could try to leap.”

Pip watched. The puddle shimmered. A dragonfly dipped a tail in it, then flew away. Barnaby unrolled his mental map and made a tiny note. “This puddle is called the Learning Puddle,” he said. “I will remember it.”

Pip took a deep breath. Then she did something brave: she did not try to fly. She practiced landing. She jumped from a low stone, fluttered, and let her feet touch the dirt. Thump. Again. Thump. Again. It was not exciting. It was not sky-touching. But every time she landed, she felt the ground catch her, soft and sure.

By the time the sun tilted toward afternoon, the Learning Puddle had shrunk by a thumb’s width. And Pip had learned to land without tumbling.

“Now,” said Barnaby. “Try the wind.”

Pip spread her wings. A breeze rustled the oak leaves. She jumped, flapped, and rose—not straight up, but on a gentle slant, like a feather on a breath. The wind lifted her. She climbed past the hose, past the rosebush, past the Big Wobbler. For a moment, she was high enough to see the whole garden: the wet, shining roofs, the blue puddles scattered like jewels, and a tiny snail on a stone, looking up.

Then she remembered. Landing.

She tilted her wings, slowed herself, and aimed for the Learning Puddle. Her feet touched the edge. Not a splash. Not a stumble. Just a soft, perfect landing in the shallow water, which rippled once and then was still.

Barnaby smiled. “You did it.”

Pip was quiet. She looked at the puddle, now just a damp spot. “It’s almost gone,” she said softly.

“Yes,” said Barnaby. “Puddles are not forever. But the learning stays.”

Pip looked at the sky and then at the ground. “I think,” she said, “I will fly again tomorrow. But first, I want to draw a map with you.”

And so they did. Barnaby showed her how every puddle holds a story: how the Deep One by the rosebush had once been a footprint, how the Tiny Twinklers only appeared after the hardest rain. Pip listened. She did not hop ahead. She stayed beside the snail, step by slow step.

That evening, when the last puddle shrank to a dark gleam, Pip perched on a low branch. She had not touched the yellow leaf. But she had touched something better: the patience to wait, the bravery to learn, and the knowledge that landing is not the end of flying—it is the beginning.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Opens “Pollinator Path” to Inspire Neighborhood-Wide Habitat Connections**

**HARBORLEAF, CA – July 1, 2026** – The Harborleaf Botanical Garden will officially open its new “Pollinator Path” public education trail on **Sunday, July 12, 2026**, transforming a 0.8-mile loop in the east meadow into an immersive, accessible learning experience focused on the needs of bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds. Designed in direct response to visitor requests for practical, actionable planting guidance, the trail features native-plant demonstration beds, a night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, and shaded resting points.

The Pollinator Path occupies the east meadow loop, a paved route suitable for wheelchairs, strollers, and visitors of all mobility levels. It is intended to serve as a living classroom that demonstrates how small, intentional plant choices in a home garden, balcony, or community plot can create linked corridors of habitat across urban and suburban neighborhoods.

Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden, emphasized the trail’s core educational mission.

“We hear from visitors regularly that they want to help pollinators but don’t know where to start—what to plant, when to plant, or which flowers support specific species through different seasons,” Ortiz said. “The Pollinator Path is designed to show, in a very concrete way, that small planting decisions—a pot of native milkweed on an apartment balcony, a patch of goldenrod in a front yard—can add up to create connected habitats that stretch across entire neighborhoods.”

The trail includes multiple demonstration beds planted exclusively with California-native species known to support local pollinators. Stop signs along the route identify host plants for caterpillars, nectar sources for adult butterflies and bees, and larval food plants for moths. A dedicated night-blooming flower station will be illuminated during evening hours to showcase plants such as evening primrose and moonflower, which attract nocturnal pollinators including certain moth species and bats.

Interpretive content is presented through tactile signage, with raised text, braille, and embossed illustrations of key plant and insect species. Youth observation cards—available free at the trailhead—guide younger visitors through a scavenger-hunt-style activity, prompting them to note which pollinators they see at each station. Six shaded resting points, each equipped with a small bench and a shade sail, offer respite along the loop and double as quiet observation spots for photography or sketching.

A volunteer-led weekend walk will launch on the opening day and continue every Saturday and Sunday through the end of the growing season. These guided tours, led by trained community educators, will provide in-depth explanations of plant-pollinator relationships, seasonal bloom cycles, and simple garden modifications such as leaving bare soil for ground-nesting bees or avoiding pesticide use.

The Pollinator Path was developed following a visitor survey conducted by the garden in 2024, in which a majority of respondents expressed interest in learning more about “practical, low-cost ways to support pollinators at home.” The garden’s horticulture team curated the plant list based on regional ecological data from local native-plant societies and academic research on urban pollinator movement.

The trail also features a small “seed swap station” near the trailhead, where visitors can leave and take seeds of native pollinator-friendly plants in exchange. The station is stocked with species that are easy to grow in containers or small garden plots, reinforcing the trail’s message that habitat creation is accessible to anyone.

The east meadow loop is part of the garden’s existing network of walking paths, but the Pollinator Path represents the first themed educational trail dedicated entirely to pollinator ecology. The garden expects the trail to become a primary draw for school field trips, community garden groups, and individual home gardeners alike.

“If every person who walks this trail chooses just one plant that supports a pollinator, the collective impact across this region could be transformative,” Ortiz added.

The Harborleaf Botanical Garden is a public, nonprofit educational institution that has operated since 1978. It manages more than 40 acres of display gardens, natural habitats, and conservation collections. Admission to the Pollinator Path is included with regular garden entry. The garden is open daily from 9 a.m. to sunset.

**###**

**Media Contact:**

Elena Torres  
Communications Director  
Harborleaf Botanical Garden  
555-0199  
etorres@harborleafgarden.org

*For opening-day media access, interviews with Maya Ortiz, or high-resolution images, please contact Elena Torres by July 9.*

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The laundromat on Harbor Street smelled of bleach and salt, even in the dark. The power had cut twenty minutes ago, plunging the small coin-operated space into a grainy twilight lit only by the weak coastal moon through the front window. That was when the door chimed, and Lena walked in with a basket of damp towels.

She stopped when she saw him.

Miles was sitting on the cracked vinyl bench against the far wall, his elbows on his knees, a battery-powered lantern glowing amber on the floor beside him. The lantern cast long shadows up his face, sharpening the hollows under his cheekbones and the silver threading his dark hair. He looked older. Tired in a way that wasn’t just from the late hour.

“I was across the street,” she said, because the silence was already too heavy. “At the diner. Saw the lights go out here. Thought maybe someone might need a hand.”

It was a lie. She’d seen the lantern through the glass from the parking lot. She’d recognized his silhouette before she even knew why her feet were carrying her toward the door.

Miles didn’t stand. He just looked at her, and his eyes were the same deep brown she remembered, only now they held a careful distance, like a man who had learned to keep his hurt within reach.

“Lena,” he said. Not a question. A fact.

She set her basket down on the folding table. The towels were still warm from her own dryer at home, half-dried, useless now. She’d been meaning to bring them back for a proper cycle before the outage turned them damp and sour.

“You’re not from here,” she said.

“I’m not.”

“Visiting?”

“Staying for a few days. My mother’s house. She died in the spring.”

The words landed flat and clean, like stones dropped into still water. Lena folded her arms across her chest. The night air was cool through the cracked window, raising goosebumps on her bare arms. “I’m sorry, Miles. She was a kind woman.”

“She was.”

The lantern flickered once, steadied. Outside, the street was quiet, the only sound the distant sigh of the tide against the jetty. Lena had moved away from this town twelve years ago, and she had never told him. She had simply packed her car one morning while he was at work, left a note for the landlord, and driven three states north. The note had not included a forwarding address.

She had told herself it was for the best. They had been neighbors, nothing more. Two people in their mid-twenties living in adjacent units above a closed bakery, sharing a wall so thin she could hear him brushing his teeth. They had never kissed. They had never touched beyond a polite hand on the shoulder, a brief arm when the stairs were icy. But they had talked until three in the morning on his fire escape, watching the harbor lights. She had known the exact pitch of his laugh, the way he said her name with a soft, falling inflection, like a question he already knew the answer to.

And she had left without a word.

“You could have called,” he said now, his voice quiet but not accusing. It was worse than accusation. It was a statement of simple, unadorned truth.

“I know.”

“I looked for you. For about six months. Then I stopped.”

She nodded. Her throat felt tight, the way it did before a storm. She unlatched one of the dryers, pulled out a damp towel, and began folding it with mechanical precision. The fabric was cool and heavy in her hands. It gave her something to do besides look at him.

“I didn’t know how to say goodbye,” she said. “And I couldn’t stay.”

“You never told me why.”

“No.”

He didn’t push. He never had. That was the worst part—he had always given her space, always respected the walls she put up, even when she was building them right in front of him. And she had used that kindness against him. She had turned his patience into a door she could walk through without looking back.

The towel was folded into a perfect rectangle. She set it on the table and reached for another.

“You left a voicemail,” he said.

She stopped. Her hands stilled on the damp terry cloth. “What?”

“The night before you left. You called me. I was at work, I didn’t pick up. You left a voicemail.” He reached into his jacket pocket and pulled out a phone. The screen was dark. He didn’t turn it on. “I never deleted it.”

Lena’s chest tightened. She remembered that call. She remembered standing in her empty apartment, the walls bare, a single box by the door. She had dialed his number, heard it ring, and when the voicemail picked up, she had opened her mouth and said nothing. The recording was forty-three seconds of silence, ending with the soft click of her hanging up.

“I didn’t say anything,” she whispered.

“You didn’t need to.” He put the phone away. “I knew what it meant. But I kept it anyway. Because for a long time, it was all I had.”

The lantern hummed. The dryers ticked as they cooled. Lena pressed the folded towel to her chest like a shield.

“I was scared,” she said. The words came out raw, stripped of the careful composure she had worn for twelve years. “Of how much I wanted to stay. Of how much I wanted you to ask me to. And I knew if you asked, I would say yes. And I couldn’t. I had a mother who needed me, a sister who was sick, obligations that didn’t fit into a fire escape conversation at two in the morning. So I left before you could make me choose.”

The confession hung between them, fragile as the lantern’s glass.

Miles picked up the lantern and stood. He was taller than she remembered, or maybe she had forgotten how to measure height against memory. He walked over to the folding table and set the lantern down between them. Its warm light pooled on the damp towels, illuminating the creases in his shirt, the slight tremor in his hand.

“I would have gone with you,” he said. “If you had asked.”

“I know.”

“I would have followed you anywhere, Lena. That’s what you didn’t understand. It wouldn’t have been a choice for you to make alone.”

She closed her eyes. The smell of bleach and salt and the faint, unplaceable scent of him—laundry soap, coffee, the old wood of his apartment—pressed in around her.

“I’m sorry,” she said.

It was not enough. It was a cool towel after a twelve-year drought. But she said it anyway.

He looked at her for a long moment. Then he reached into his pocket, pulled out his phone, and held it out to her.

“Take it.”

She looked at the black screen. “What?”

“The voicemail. Delete it. I never could.”

Her fingers brushed his as she took the phone. His skin was warm. She held his gaze for one breath, two, then unlocked the screen. The voicemail app was open. One message from a number she no longer had. Forty-three seconds.

She pressed play.

Silence. Her own breathing, faint and distant. The rustle of a coat. Then the soft, final click of her goodbyeless ending.

She deleted it.

She handed the phone back. Their fingers touched again, just briefly.

The power came back with a shuddering hum. The fluorescent lights above flickered, buzzed, and steadied, flooding the laundromat with harsh white light. The dryers began to turn. The world resumed.

Miles put his phone away. He picked up the lantern. His hand brushed hers once more, a ghost of a gesture, and then he walked to the door.

“Goodnight, Lena.”

She didn’t say goodnight. She just watched him go, the damp towel still pressed to her chest, the lantern’s glow fading into the dark street.

And she let the silence speak for her, just as she had all those years ago.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# Rate Limiting for Multi-Tenant APIs: Balancing Protection and Fairness Across Small Teams and Enterprises

## 1. Foundational Concepts: Authentication, Authorization, Quotas, and Rate Limits

Before designing any rate-limiting system, we must disambiguate four distinct but often conflated concepts:

- **Authentication** answers "Who are you?" – verifying identity via API keys, OAuth tokens, or mTLS certificates. It is a prerequisite for all subsequent checks.
- **Authorization** answers "What are you allowed to do?" – controlling access to specific resources or actions (e.g., "Can this tenant read financial reports?"). Authorization does not constrain *how many* requests.
- **Quotas** are long-term capacity entitlements, typically measured over a billing cycle (e.g., 100,000 API calls per month). They govern *total usage* and are often enforced via periodic resets. Quotas are contractual; exceeding them usually results in billing overage or feature degradation.
- **Rate limits** are short-term *velocity* controls, measured in requests per second, minute, or hour. They protect infrastructure from traffic spikes, prevent abuse, and ensure system stability. Unlike quotas, rate limits reset quickly and are about *instantaneous load*, not cumulative usage.

The critical insight: **authentication occurs first, then authorization, then rate limiting, then quota enforcement.** Rate limiting should never be a substitute for authorization, and quotas should never be used to prevent a DDoS. They are complementary layers.

## 2. Token Bucket vs. Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window: Practical Trade-offs

### Fixed Window
Divide time into discrete buckets (e.g., 60-second windows). Count requests per window. When the counter hits the limit, reject further requests until the next window.

- **Pros**: Extremely simple to implement and reason about; low memory overhead (one counter per key).
- **Cons**: **The "burst at the edge" problem.** If a tenant sends 100 requests at the last millisecond of window N, then 100 more at the first millisecond of window N+1, they delivered 200 requests in two milliseconds—effectively doubling allowed throughput. This is catastrophic for downstream services.
- **Best for**: Mock or debugging endpoints where precision is irrelevant.

### Sliding Window Log
Maintain a sorted list of timestamps for each tenant. Every request adds a timestamp and removes timestamps older than the window duration. If the list size exceeds the limit, reject.

- **Pros**: Smooth, exact enforcement—no edge bursts.
- **Cons**: Memory and time complexity O(n) per request in naive implementations. Even with binary search and pruning, large tenants with high throughput force pathological storage growth.
- **Best for**: Low-traffic APIs or tenants with very small limits.

### Sliding Window Counter
A hybrid approach: track the current window's count and the previous window's count. Estimate the rate at any point as:  
`previous_count * (1 - elapsed_fraction) + current_count`.  
Reject if the estimate exceeds the limit.

- **Pros**: Low memory (two counters per key), O(1) per request, eliminates the edge burst problem (within reasonable approximation).
- **Cons**: Not exact—a burst exactly at a window boundary can exceed the limit by up to one request. Usually acceptable.
- **Best for**: General-purpose production systems requiring both accuracy and efficiency.

### Token Bucket
Maintain a bucket of capacity tokens that refill at a constant rate (e.g., 10 tokens per second). Each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, reject.

- **Pros**: Smooth rate enforcement; allows natural bursts up to bucket depth (e.g., 100 tokens permits a 100-request burst at T=0). Simple to reason about with traffic shaping.
- **Cons**: Requires careful configuration of bucket depth and refill rate. If depth is too large, one noisy tenant can still overwhelm shared resources.
- **Best for**: APIs where controlled bursting is a feature (e.g., webhook callbacks) or where traffic patterns are irregular.

**Practical recommendation**: Use **sliding window counter** for most endpoints—it is a pragmatic compromise. Use **token bucket** only for specific "burst-friendly" endpoints (e.g., batch POST endpoints) where you explicitly want to allow spikes drawn from a credit bank.

## 3. Avoiding Punishing an Entire Tenant for One Noisy Integration

A multi-tenant API serves thousands of integrations within a single tenant: third-party webhooks, internal services, cron jobs, developer tools, etc. If rate limits are applied exclusively at the tenant level, a single misconfigured script can saturate the entire tenant's allowance—blocking billing, reporting, and critical customer workflows.

**Solution: Hierarchical rate limiting with shared and per-integration buckets.**

- **Per-tenant global limit** (e.g., 100 req/s): enforced at a coarse granularity using a sliding window counter. This protects your infrastructure.
- **Per-integration sub-limit** (e.g., 20 req/s per API key or per application identifier): enforced using the same algorithm but keyed by a finer identifier (e.g., API key hash + path prefix). This isolates noisy actors.
- **Hierarchical admission**: Allow a request only if *both* the per-integration bucket and the per-tenant bucket have capacity. This ensures no single integration can exhaust the tenant's global allocation, while still capping total tenant load.

Additionally, implement **minimum guaranteed capacity** for critical endpoints (e.g., billing, health checks). Reserve a small "emergency quota" at the tenant level that bypasses per-integration limits—but never at the infrastructure level.

**Example scenario**: Tenant A has 10 integrations, each independently limited to 10 req/s. Integration X goes rogue and hits 10 req/s continuously. Integration Y can still operate at its own 10 req/s because per-integration limits are independent. However, if 50 integrations all reach 10 req/s simultaneously, the per-tenant limit of 100 req/s will block them—protecting your backend from 500 req/s.

## 4. Headers and Error Messages to Return

Clear, standardised feedback is essential for client-side developers to handle throttling gracefully. Use HTTP headers consistent with the IETF standard (RFC 6585) and common industry practices:

### Response Headers (on every request)
- `X-RateLimit-Limit`: The maximum number of requests allowed per window (e.g., `100`).
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining`: The number of requests remaining in the current window (e.g., `87`).
- `X-RateLimit-Reset`: The UTC epoch time (seconds) when the current window resets. Helps clients compute backoff precisely.

### When Rejected (HTTP 429 Too Many Requests)
- `Retry-After`: Seconds the client should wait before retrying (e.g., `3`). Use an integer; do not return fractional seconds.

### Error Body (JSON)
Return a consistent structure:
```json
{
  "error": "rate_limit_exceeded",
  "message": "You have exceeded the rate limit. Please retry after the specified time.",
  "retry_after_seconds": 3,
  "limit": 100,
  "remaining": 0,
  "reset_at": 1699826400
}
```

**Crucial detail**: For multi-tenant isolation, include which scope was exceeded (`tenant` vs `integration`) in the error body:
```json
"scope": "integration",
"integration_id": "key-abc123"
```
This avoids confusion when a tenant's global limit has capacity but their individual key is blocked.

Do **not** return stack traces, internal configuration details, or "credit" information that reveals your underlying algorithm. Keep the interface stable even if you change the implementation.

## 5. Observability: Metrics, Logs, and Alerts

Rate limits are only useful if you know they are working as intended. Observability must answer: *Are we rejecting legitimate traffic? Are we protecting downstream services?*

### Key Metrics (exposed via your monitoring platform)
- **`rate_limit.hits{scope="tenant", decision="allowed"}`**: Count all accepted requests, tagged by tenant tier, integration ID, and endpoint.
- **`rate_limit.hits{scope="integration", decision="rejected"}`**: Count rejected requests per integration. Spike = noisy actor.
- **`rate_limit.hits{scope="tenant", decision="rejected"}`**: Count tenant-level rejections. This should be rare; a spike indicates a badly distributed sub-limit configuration.
- **`downstream.latency_p99`**: Critical—if latency increases while rejection rates are low, your rate limits may be too generous.
- **`rate_limit.bucket_depth_remaining`**: Track average remaining capacity per tenant over time. Persistent near-zero values suggest you are operating at the edge of capacity.

### Logs
Log every rate-limited request with structured fields: `tenant_id`, `integration_id`, `route`, `limit_type` (tenant vs. integration), `limit_value`, `retry_after`. Do not log successful requests by default to avoid explosion.

### Alerts
- **P1**: Rate limit rejections exceed 5% of all requests for 5 minutes → indicates either an attack or misconfigured limits.
- **P2**: Any single tenant is responsible for >50% of all rejections → investigate for runaway integration.
- **P3**: Average `retry_after` for rejected requests exceeds 30 seconds → clients are retrying too aggressively; check your `Retry-After` accuracy.
- **P4**: Per-integration rejections persistently account for >90% of all rejections → your per-integration limits may be too aggressive; consider raising them.

## Recommended Baseline Design

For a mid-scale multi-tenant API (10,000 tenants, mixed small teams and enterprise), implement:

1. **Algorithm**: Sliding window counter for both tenant and integration limits. Use a fast in-memory store (e.g., sharded in-process map or external cache) for low-latency decision-making.
2. **Hierarchy**: 
   - Tenant global limit: 5x the sum of per-integration limits to allow some natural headroom.
   - Per-integration limit: 20% of tenant global limit, with a minimum floor of 5 req/s.
3. **Capacity reservation**: Reserve 10% of tenant capacity for a priority endpoint (e.g., `/health`, `/billing`). This is implemented as a second per-tenant bucket that is checked before the general bucket, with its own sub-limit.
4. **Headers**: Include all three `X-RateLimit-*` headers plus `Retry-After` on 429s. Return JSON error bodies with `scope` field.
5. **Observability**: Emit metrics for allowed/rejected counts at both scopes, track downstream latency, and alert on rejection rate >5% or single-tenant domination.

This baseline avoids punishing entire tenants for one noisy integration while providing sharp visibility into traffic patterns. Adjust limits based on actual observed capacity planning data—not theoretical maxima.

请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters should never have been written in the first place. And among those that are written, a good number suffer from incomplete information or poor organization—turning what could have been settled in two letters into six or eight rounds of correspondence. A good letter begins with clear thinking and careful planning. While a sales letter may take weeks to refine, a routine business reply can be done in just a few minutes. What matters is not how long it took to write, but whether the final version accomplishes its task with precision.

There are many rules for evaluating a business letter, but only two are truly essential: first, the letter must make your meaning clear to the recipient; second, it must build goodwill for your company. The best letters achieve both in the shortest, most courteous, and most complete way possible. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it—even if it's simply a thank-you to a client. Too much effort is wasted on aimless advertisements, ineffective solicitations, and unnecessary correspondence. These fill pages and tie up typists and paper without advancing anything.

A good letter should not draw attention to its own prose; it should put the message front and center. A publishing house once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Recipients praised the writing—but no one placed an order. This shows that when language steals focus from the message, even elegant writing fails as business communication. The paper, letterhead, layout, and paragraphs should all be clean, restrained, and clear. Overly flashy design or forced novelty in format only distracts.

Business writing should also respect the reader’s time. Clear subject lines, proper salutations, topic-based paragraphs, a specific request, and complete contact details build trust far more effectively than elaborate phrasing. If a letter requires a response, the writer should state clearly by when the reply is needed, what information is required, and what the next step will be. In this way, the letter does more than convey an attitude—it moves the matter forward.

请将下面的繁体中文寓言/哲理片段翻译成英文，目标读者为青少年读者。要求：保留想象力和节奏；遇到典故不要长篇解释；译文应清楚、流畅，适合作为儿童文学或寓言文本阅读。

版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
**The Story of the Great Fish and the Giant Bird**  

In the dark, cold sea of the north, there lived a fish called Kun. Nobody knew how huge Kun was—thousands of miles long, maybe more. Then, with a twist and a turn, Kun changed into a bird. Its name was Peng. Peng’s back was so wide you couldn’t see where it ended. When it spread its wings and flew, they looked like clouds hanging across the sky.  

This bird waited for the great winds of the ocean to carry it all the way to the southern sea, the Celestial Lake.  

A book called *Odd Tales* says: “When Peng flies to the southern sea, it stirs up the water for three thousand miles. Then it rides a whirlwind up to a height of ninety thousand miles, leaving on the breath of the sixth-month wind.”  

What a sight! Like wild horses galloping across the sky, like dust dancing in the sunlight—all living things blow their breath upon each other.  

The sky looks blue. But is that its real color? Or is it just so far away you can’t see the end? When Peng looks down from high above, the world must look just as far and strange to it.  

If water isn’t deep enough, it can’t float a big boat. Pour a cup of water on a little hole in the floor, and a mustard leaf can sail on it like a boat. But put a cup in the same puddle—it gets stuck. The water is too shallow.  

The same goes for wind. If the wind isn’t strong enough, it can’t lift a giant bird’s wings. So when Peng flies at ninety thousand miles high, the wind is under it, strong and thick. Only then can it ride the wind, with the blue sky on its back, and nothing blocking its way. Only then can it head south.  

A little cicada and a small dove laughed at Peng. “We fly straight up as fast as we can, and if we don’t make it to a tree branch, we just drop to the ground. Why does that bird need to fly ninety thousand miles south?”  

But if you’re just going to the woods outside the town, you can eat three meals and come back still full. If you’re going a hundred miles, you need to grind grain the night before. If you’re going a thousand miles, you need to pack food for three months.  

Those two little creatures—what do they know?  

Little knowledge can’t compare to great knowledge. A short life can’t understand a long one. A morning mushroom doesn’t know about the full moon. A summer insect doesn’t know about winter. That’s a short life.  

But in the south of Chu, there is a sacred tree called Mingling. For it, five hundred years is just one spring, and another five hundred years is just one autumn. In ancient times, there was a huge tree called Dachun. For it, eight thousand years was spring, and eight thousand years was autumn.  

But then there was old Peng Zu, who lived for hundreds of years and became famous. People compare themselves to him—isn’t that sad?  

Even the wise king Tang asked the same questions. Far in the barren north, there is a dark sea—the Celestial Lake. In it lives a fish thousands of miles wide, and nobody knows how long. Its name is Kun. And there lives a bird named Peng, with a back like Mount Tai and wings like clouds hanging from the sky. It whirls up to ninety thousand miles, above the clouds, carrying the blue sky on its back. Only then does it head south.  

A little quail laughed at it. “Where does it think it’s going? I jump up and fly a few feet, then land again. I flutter happily among the reeds and bushes. That’s the best kind of flying! So why does that bird want to go so far?”  

That’s the difference between small and big.  

People who are clever enough to do one small job, or good enough to please one village, or wise enough to serve one king—they look at themselves the same way the little quail does. And Song Rongzi laughed at them.  

He wouldn’t get excited if the whole world praised him, and he wouldn’t get upset if the whole world blamed him. He knew the difference between “inside” and “outside,” and understood what true honor and shame were. But even he hadn’t reached the highest level yet.  

Then there was Liezi, who could ride the wind. Light and graceful, he’d travel for fifteen days and then return. He wasn’t chasing happiness or luck. But even he depended on the wind.  

What about someone who rides the true Way of heaven and earth, who harnesses the six changing winds, and wanders freely through the endless universe? What does that person need to depend on?  

So it is said:  
The Perfect One has no self.  
The Spirit Being has no achievements.  
The True Sage has no name.

messages.content:
请将下面的中文新闻稿素材翻译成英文。目标读者为普通英语新闻读者。要求：准确、自然、新闻稿风格；不要添加事实；保留机构名的含义，可译为 U.S. Geological Survey；不要使用夸张宣传语。

素材来源：改写自 U.S. Geological Survey 水循环图发布新闻稿
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain; this Chinese source is a benchmark rewrite based on that public-domain material.

【待处理素材】
美国地质调查局发布新版水循环教学图，帮助教育者更完整地解释地球水在自然系统与人类活动之间的流动。新版图示取代了自2000年以来被大量课堂使用的旧版本。旧图多以一个大循环表现蒸发、降水、径流等自然过程，而新版把人类用水、土地利用、蓄水工程、灌溉和地下水开采等因素纳入同一张图中，展示许多小循环如何彼此连接、相互影响。

该图的开发团队咨询了一百多名教育工作者和三十多名水文学专家，并参考了美国地质调查局长期积累的水文数据。新版图示不仅说明水如何在大气、地表和地下储存、转移和改变形态，也强调水量、水质和水到达时间对可用水资源的影响。例如，降水并不会简单地从天空落下再回到海洋；一部分水会进入土壤和含水层，一部分会被植物吸收并通过蒸腾回到空气中，一部分会被水库、农田、城市排水系统和家庭用水改变路径。

设计者采用信息设计原则，让读者能顺着水的路径观察沿海平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地和农业盆地之间的联系。图中还强调，不同区域的用水选择会影响下游水量和水质，因此理解水循环不能只看自然过程，也要观察人类活动如何改变水的储存位置、移动速度和清洁程度。新版图示最初提供英文和西班牙文版本，预期将由使用者翻译成更多语言。发布方表示，这一工具旨在帮助学生、教师和自然资源管理者以更清晰的方式理解水的复杂旅程，并认识到日常用水选择会影响水资源的可持续利用。

message.content:
**U.S. Geological Survey Releases Updated Water Cycle Diagram for Classrooms**

The U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) has released a new educational diagram of the water cycle, designed to help educators offer a more complete explanation of how Earth’s water moves through both natural systems and human activity.

The new diagram replaces a version that had been widely used in classrooms since 2000. While the older diagram primarily depicted natural processes such as evaporation, precipitation, and runoff within a single large cycle, the updated version incorporates human water use, land use, water storage infrastructure, irrigation, and groundwater extraction. It shows how many smaller cycles are connected and influence one another.

The development team consulted more than 100 educators and over 30 hydrology experts, and drew upon long-term hydrological data collected by the USGS. The updated diagram not only illustrates how water is stored, transferred, and transformed in the atmosphere, on the surface, and underground, but also emphasizes how water quantity, water quality, and the timing of water availability affect usable water resources.

For example, precipitation does not simply fall from the sky and return to the ocean. Some water enters the soil and aquifers, some is taken up by plants and returned to the air through transpiration, and some is diverted by reservoirs, farmland, urban drainage systems, and household use.

The designers applied principles of information design to allow readers to follow the path of water and observe connections between coastal plains, dry basins, wet basins, and agricultural basins. The diagram also highlights that water use choices in one area can affect both water quantity and quality downstream. Understanding the water cycle, therefore, requires examining not only natural processes but also how human activities change where water is stored, how fast it moves, and how clean it is.

The new diagram is initially available in English and Spanish, and is expected to be translated into additional languages by users. The USGS stated that the tool is intended to help students, teachers, and natural resource managers better understand the complex journey of water and recognize that daily water use choices affect the sustainable use of water resources.
