Write a single-file Node.js HTTP server named `server.js`. Use only built-in Node.js modules; no Express, no npm packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Task: implement a small JSON API for a help-desk ticket queue stored in a local JSON file.

Data model:
- Ticket: `id`, `subject`, `description`, `priority` (`low`, `medium`, `high`), `status` (`open`, `in_progress`, `resolved`), `created_at`, `updated_at`.

API requirements:
1. `GET /tickets` with optional query params `status` and `priority`.
2. `POST /tickets` to create a ticket. Validate required fields.
3. `GET /tickets/{id}` to fetch one ticket.
4. `PATCH /tickets/{id}` to update `subject`, `description`, `priority`, or `status`.
5. `DELETE /tickets/{id}` to delete a ticket.
6. `GET /metrics` returning counts by status and priority plus average age of open tickets in seconds.

Engineering requirements:
- Read/write a `tickets.json` file safely enough for sequential requests.
- Return JSON for all responses, including errors.
- Include CORS headers for local browser testing.
- Handle invalid JSON, unknown routes, unsupported methods, and missing IDs.
- Start on `127.0.0.1:8080` by default.

Output only the complete JavaScript code. Do not include explanations.

Create a single-file frontend app named `index.html`. Do not use external libraries, CDNs, images, or build tools. Keep the total code under 1,000 lines.

Task: build an accessible “meeting notes board” for a small team.

Functional requirements:
1. Users can add a note with title, owner, meeting date, tags, and body.
2. Notes appear as cards and can be filtered by owner, tag, and free-text search.
3. A note can be marked as “Action Required,” “Decision,” or “Reference.”
4. Users can edit and delete notes.
5. Persist all data in `localStorage`.
6. Provide a compact statistics strip: total notes, action-required count, unique owners, and most common tag.
7. Responsive layout: two-column card grid on wide screens, single column on narrow screens.
8. Basic accessibility: labels for inputs, keyboard-operable controls, visible focus states.

Output only the complete `index.html` content, including HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Do not include explanatory text.

Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary for modern business readers.

Output requirements:
1. A 100-word executive summary.
2. Five principles of effective business writing implied by the author.
3. A table with two columns: “Original-era advice” and “Modern equivalent.” Include at least five rows.
4. Identify any advice that may be dated and explain how to update it without losing the underlying principle.
5. Do not add claims that are not grounded in the passage.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” longer excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is the structured English summary for modern business readers based on the provided passage.

### Executive Summary

Half of all business letters should never be written, and most are so poorly planned that a simple transaction drags on for six to eight exchanges. Effective business writing requires clear thinking and planning. A letter succeeds only if it conveys its message clearly and builds goodwill for the firm; it must never draw attention to itself over its content. Most commercial correspondence rules are dangerous shortcuts. Standardization, not eccentricity, signals professionalism. Writers should use plain stationery, handle one subject per letter, and ensure addresses are complete and accurate. Careless errors—unsigned letters, missing enclosures, or insufficient postage—are common but costly.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing

1.  **Write Only When Necessary** – A letter should have a reason; half of all business writing is wasteful.
2.  **Plan Before You Write** – A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning, not mere typing.
3.  **Prioritize Clarity and Goodwill** – The only essential measures are getting the idea across and building goodwill for the firm.
4.  **Make the Message the Star** – No text is effective if it draws attention to itself instead of the message it delivers.
5.  **Standardize for Professionalism** – Custom has standardized the format; startling variations indicate freakishness, not originality.

### Table: Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-Era Advice | Modern Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| "Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste." | Use clean, professional digital templates and standard fonts (e.g., Calibri, Arial) without distracting backgrounds or animations. |
| "A business letter should handle only one subject." | Send one focused email per topic; avoid mixing multiple requests or updates in a single digital thread. |
| "Special care should be taken with the outside address… State abbreviations should be used sparingly." | In email, double-check the recipient's address field and use the full, correct domain name; in mail, use official two-letter state codes. |
| "Each subject… should be treated in a separate paragraph… double space between paragraphs." | Use clear headings, bullet points, and white space in emails and documents to separate distinct ideas for scannability. |
| “Gentlemen” or “Ladies” is sometimes used in salutation… “Dear Friend” is permissible. | Use the recipient’s name (e.g., “Dear Alex,”) or a neutral, inclusive greeting like “Hello Team” or “Hi [Company Name].” |

### Dated Advice and Modern Adaptation

**Dated Advice:** The passage instructs that most business letters should be typewritten by "girls who have been trained for the purpose" and that the dictator must correct "highly irresponsible" young stenographers.

**Modern Adaptation:** The underlying principle is that the *writer* retains full responsibility for the quality, accuracy, and formatting of any business correspondence, whether typed personally or managed through administrative support. Today, this means the writer should proofread their own email, check for attachments before clicking send, and use spell-check or grammar tools, regardless of who composes the final text. The principle—accountability for the final output—remains unchanged; only the role of the typist has evolved.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary
Mary Lennox, a disagreeable nine-year-old, is sent to live with her uncle at Misselthwaite Manor after her parents die in a cholera outbreak in India. Born to a neglectful mother who kept her out of sight, Mary was raised by servants who obeyed her every whim, making her tyrannical and selfish. One hot morning, her Ayah is missing, and Mary throws a tantrum. She overhears her mother speaking anxiously with a young officer about the cholera epidemic. Soon, wailing announces the Ayah’s death. As chaos erupts, Mary hides in the nursery, forgotten by everyone. She eats leftover food, drinks wine, and falls asleep, isolated and uncared for amid the tragedy.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Evidence
- **Disagreeable appearance**: She has a “thin face,” “thin body,” “thin light hair,” and “a sour expression.”
- **Tyrannical**: She beats and kicks a servant who refuses to summon her Ayah.
- **Selfish**: Her mother handed her to an Ayah to keep her “out of sight,” and servants always gave her “her own way.”
- **Ill-tempered**: Upon waking, she feels “very cross,” and mutters insults like “Pig! Daughter of Pigs!”
- **Neglected**: During the cholera crisis, she is “forgotten by everyone” and “nobody thought of her.”

### Setting and Emotional Distance
The setting—a colonial Indian bungalow during a cholera outbreak—creates emotional isolation by emphasizing Mary’s abandonment. She is physically left alone, hiding in the nursery while adults panic around her. The servants’ wailing and hurried footsteps underscore her separation from the crisis; no one includes or explains events to her. The empty dining room with hastily abandoned food highlights how she is overlooked, even as tragedy unfolds. This atmosphere of confusion and fear deepens Mary’s loneliness, as she is neither comforted nor guided, reinforcing her status as an unwanted, forgotten child.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary behaves so badly, like beating the servant? Does how she was raised explain her actions?
2. How do you think Mary feels when she hears the wailing and realizes her Ayah has died? What might she be thinking?
3. If you were in Mary’s place during the cholera outbreak, what would you want an adult to do for you?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes the constant movement of water across Earth, stored in the atmosphere, on land, and underground. Oceans hold 96% of all water (saline), while fresh water exists in lakes, rivers, ice sheets, glaciers, and snowpack. Water changes among liquid, solid, and gas forms. Key processes include evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, snowmelt, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. The sun’s energy and gravity drive this movement. Human water use, land use, and climate change impact the cycle. Understanding these effects helps promote sustainable water use.

### Concept Map (Text Form)
Sun’s energy → Evaporation/Evapotranspiration → Atmosphere (water vapor) → Precipitation → Land Surface (snow, lakes, rivers) → Snowmelt/Runoff → Streamflow → Infiltration → Groundwater (aquifers) → Groundwater discharge → Oceans, Rivers, Springs

### Key Terms
1. **Water cycle** – The continuous movement and storage of water on Earth.
2. **Evaporation** – The process where liquid water turns into water vapor due to the sun’s energy.
3. **Evapotranspiration** – The main way water moves from land surfaces and oceans into the atmosphere.
4. **Precipitation** – Rain, snow, or hail that falls from clouds due to gravity.
5. **Infiltration** – The movement of water from the surface into the ground.
6. **Groundwater recharge** – The process by which infiltrated water enters underground aquifers.
7. **Aquifer** – An underground layer where groundwater flows and is stored.
8. **Runoff** – Water that flows across the land surface toward streams and rivers.
9. **Snowpack** – Solid, frozen water stored at high elevations or near Earth’s poles.
10. **Watershed** – A large-scale area over which water moves across the land surface.

### Human Influence
Human water use, land use, and climate change affect water movement, storage, and availability. Water use alters how much water is taken from or returned to natural pools. Land use changes, such as construction or agriculture, modify infiltration, runoff, and evapotranspiration rates. Climate change influences the timing and intensity of precipitation, snowmelt, and evaporation. These human actions can disrupt natural flow paths and reduce the cleanliness or quantity of water available in different pools.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on warehouse operations and customer experience  

**Team,**  

This week has been harder than it should have been for our customers and for many of you. I’m writing with a clear, honest update about what’s happening with our shipping and support operations, what we’re doing right now, and what you should expect next week.  

**What happened**  

On Monday, we completed the planned migration of our warehouse management system (WMS). This transition is intended to improve inventory accuracy and packing efficiency over the long term, but the cutover introduced unexpected instability in how orders are routed to pick-and-pack stations.  

Specifically, the new system’s order-batching logic does not yet reliably group items by warehouse zone. Picker routes are longer than projected, which has slowed throughput by roughly 30% since Monday. As a result, a backlog of approximately 4,200 orders has accumulated across our two fulfillment centers as of this morning.  

No single team or vendor caused this. The implementation partner and our internal operations team followed the test plan; the issue surfaced only under real-world order volume. We are owning this together.  

**Customer impact**  

- **Shipping speed.** Orders placed since Monday are shipping an average of 1.5 days later than our standard 1–2 business day window. Express orders (2-day) are delayed by up to 3 days.  
- **Support volume.** Customer service ticket volume has increased 220% versus last week. The majority of tickets are “Where is my order?” inquiries, followed by requests to cancel or change shipping methods.  
- **First-contact resolution** has dropped from 87% to 62%, primarily because our support agents cannot see real-time pick updates from the new WMS.  

**What we are doing today**  

We have three immediate priorities:  

1. **Clear the backlog.** Our ops team has added a second afternoon pick wave and authorized overtime for all warehouse associates through Saturday. We have also opened a temporary overflow line at the third-party logistics partner we use for holiday surges.  
2. **Restore visibility for support.** Our engineering team deployed a workaround this morning that pulls pick status from the WMS every 15 minutes into the support dashboard. It is not real-time, but it will reduce the number of “I don’t know” responses agents have to give. We expect full API integration by Tuesday.  
3. **Prioritize in-transit orders.** Customers who ordered between Monday and Wednesday and selected express shipping will receive a $10 credit automatically. Product teams are updating order status pages to show the new estimated delivery date (not the original promise).  

**What will change next week**  

- **Order routing rollback.** On Sunday night, we will revert the order-batching logic to our previous zone-based algorithm. This means Monday’s ship speed should return to normal. The new WMS will still handle inventory and receiving—just not pick routing—until we can run a controlled test with lower volume.  
- **Support staffing.** We are bringing in three temporary agents from our overflow vendor for Monday through Friday. Chat wait times should drop below 4 minutes by Wednesday.  
- **Transparency for customers.** Starting Monday, the order confirmation email will include a note: *“We are currently shipping orders 1–2 days later than usual. You will receive a tracking link as soon as your package leaves the warehouse.”* We will remove this note once we hit two consecutive days of on-time shipping.  

**How to escalate urgent issues**  

If you encounter a situation that cannot wait for normal channel response (e.g., a retail partner threatening to delist, a customs issue, or a safety problem at the warehouse), use this protocol:  

- **Level 1 escalation:** Your direct manager or shift lead.  
- **Level 2 escalation (within 2 hours if unresolved):** The on-call ops leader – **Maya Chen (maya.chen@company.com)** – who is reachable 24/7 this week.  
- **Level 3 escalation (urgent customer or press risk):** Me. Email or Slack me directly with the subject line “ESC – [issue].” I will respond within 30 minutes between 6 a.m. and 10 p.m. ET.  

**To frontline teams in particular**  

You are the ones who absorb this pressure directly. Every warehouse associate who is moving boxes under hot lights in extra shifts. Every customer service agent who is reading a complaint that was not caused by anything you did. I know this is tiring. We are not asking you to “stay positive.” We are asking you to keep doing what you can, tell your manager when you need support, and know that the root problem is being fixed—not just talked about.  

**Manager talking points (for your team meetings)**  

If your team asks what to tell customers or teammates who are frustrated, you can use these four bullets:  

- Our WMS migration had a rollover issue that slowed order picking. We have added extra shifts and a backup warehouse line to clear the backlog by Saturday.  
- Shipping delays are 1–2 days for standard orders, 2–3 days for express. Any customer who chose express shipping this week will get a $10 credit automatically—no need to request it.  
- Support agents now see updated pick status every 15 minutes. Wait times are longer than normal; we have added temporary agents and expect wait times under 4 minutes by Wednesday.  
- We will roll back the problematic pick routing this Sunday night. Shipping speed should return to normal next Monday. We will keep everyone posted on progress.  

**Final note**  

This is a real problem with a real timeline and real owners. I will send another update next Thursday with a full postmortem and metrics on recovery. Until then, please direct any questions to your manager, to Maya, or to me.  

Thanks for holding the line this week.  

— [Your Name]  
COO, [Company Name]

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
Here is a story for children ages 7–10.

---

### The Puddle Map and the Racing Sparrow

The garden after a summer rain was a world of mirrors. Every curled leaf held a trembling drop, and the fat, happy worms had turned the soil into dark glittering ribbons. But for a very careful snail named Silas, the most important thing was the puddles.

Silas did not hurry. He had a round, spiraled shell the color of caramel, and he carried a tiny, rolled-up leaf, which was his map. On the map, drawn with a blade of grass dipped in berry juice, were the shapes of every puddle in the garden. There was the Big Shoe-Splash near the gate, the three Tiny Teardrops under the rose bush, and the Long, Wiggly Worm-Print by the shed. Silas knew them all by heart.

“A puddle is a promise,” he would say, touching his map with a wet, gentle feeler. “It will be there, but not forever. A careful snail remembers.”

High above, in the cherry tree, a young sparrow named Piper was feeling exactly the opposite. She did not want to be careful. She wanted to fly.

Piper had soft, fluffy feathers and eyes as bright as new pennies. Her mother had taught her to hop from branch to branch, and to flutter her wings to slow her fall. “Learning to land is the bravest part of flying,” her mother always chirped.

But Piper didn’t think so. Landing meant stopping. Stopping meant being still. And being still was the most boring thing in the whole world. She wanted to feel the wind swoop her up, to race the clouds, to zoom over the sparkling puddles like a tiny, feathered rocket.

That afternoon, the sun burned away the last of the rain clouds. The puddles gleamed like silver coins. Piper perched on the lowest branch, staring at the Big Shoe-Splash puddle far below.

“I could do it,” she whispered. “I could fly from here to the gate. Just one big, glorious swoop. No landing. Just flying.”

She saw Silas the snail, slowly, slowly, making his way around the edge of the puddle, dabbing his map with a drop of water to check its size.

“Silly snail,” Piper chirped. “You’ll miss the whole point of the rain! The sun is out! It’s time to fly!”

Silas looked up with his stalk-eyes. “The sun is out,” he agreed slowly. “That is why I am checking my map. A puddle dries from the edges in. Soon, this one will be the shape of a comma, not a shoe. I must mark it before it changes.”

“Changes? It’s a puddle! It’s the same thing!” Piper laughed, and before her sensible mind could stop her, she jumped.

She didn’t flutter. She didn’t brake. She just spread her wings and let the wind take her.

For one glorious second, she was a rocket. The garden blurred into green and gold. The air rushing under her wings was the best feeling in the world.

But then she needed to turn. She tried to flap, but she was going too fast. She saw the Big Shoe-Splash puddle rushing up to meet her, and she squeezed her eyes shut.

*SPLAT!*

Piper didn’t land. She crashed. She tumbled into the cool water, coming up with a start, sputtering and covered in mud. One wing drooped, and her tail feathers were a soggy mess.

From the edge of the puddle, she heard a tiny, patient voice.

“Oh dear. You are very wet.”

It was Silas. He hadn’t run away. He had simply paused, his map safe in his curl.

“I tried to fly,” Piper chirped miserably, shivering. “I just… forgot the end part.”

“I notice,” said Silas kindly. He didn’t say ‘I told you so.’ He just tilted his shell. “Shall I show you how I cross a puddle?”

Piper was too tired and too embarrassed to argue. She watched as Silas didn’t try to zoom across. He put one slimy, careful foot into the water. Then he stopped. He felt the edge of the puddle with his feelers. Then he pulled his foot back.

“The puddle is deeper than it looks right here,” he said. “But look—over by the daisy, it is as thin as a dime. That’s the safe place to cross.”

Piper blinked. “How do you know that?”

“Because I watched it dry yesterday. And the day before. And the day before that. A puddle is not just water. It is a story. It shrinks. It grows with the next rain. Each day, it is a little different. To cross it safely, you have to be patient enough to read its story.”

Piper looked at the puddle. She had only seen a splashy obstacle. Silas saw a map of tiny changes. She looked at her own ruffled wings.

“My mom said landing is the bravest part,” Piper whispered.

Silas nodded. “My map says the same thing. Going fast is easy. Being still—waiting to see which way the water will go, or how the wind will blow when you try to come down—that takes a different kind of brave.”

Just then, the sun came out extra strong. A warm, dry breeze ruffled the garden.

Piper felt her feathers begin to dry. She hopped slowly to the edge of the puddle, the way Silas had shown her. She didn’t fly. She just sat. She watched the water. She watched a tiny ant navigate a floating leaf. She watched the puddle’s edge slowly, slowly creep inward, shrinking by the width of a hair.

“It is drying,” she whispered.

“From the edges in,” Silas confirmed.

Piper waited. She waited a long, patient minute. Then she fluffed her wings. She fluttered them gently, testing the air. The wind wasn’t a wild rocket anymore. It was a warm, soft hand that nudged her gently upward. She rose just a few inches, hovered, and then dropped lightly—softly—right beside Silas on the dry moss. Her feet touched down without a splash.

She had landed. It was her first real landing.

She looked at the puddle, then at Silas’s map.

“I see,” she said. “The map shows where the deep parts are. So you know where to be careful.”

“And where to be brave enough to wait,” said Silas, rolling up his precious leaf. “Bravery comes in all speeds, my friend. Sometimes, the bravest thing in the world is to go slow.”

Piper did not fly in a big swoop that day. She practiced hopping to the edge of the puddle, waiting, feeling the wind, and landing gently, over and over. She didn’t feel bored. She felt proud.

As the last ray of sun dried the Big Shoe-Splash into a small, dark comma, Piper tucked her head under her wing, cozy and warm. Beside her on the moss, Silas was already asleep, his little map tucked safely under his shell.

The garden was quiet. The puddle was gone, but the story of the rain stayed in Piper’s heart. She had learned that sometimes, the biggest journey isn't about how fast you go, but how carefully you choose the moment to stop.

Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Opens Pollinator Path, a New Educational Trail for Wildlife-Friendly Gardening**

**HARBORLEAF, CA – July 1, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden will open its new “Pollinator Path” public education trail on July 12, 2026, offering visitors a dedicated outdoor classroom focused on the role of native plants in supporting bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds. The 0.8-mile accessible loop in the east meadow is designed to provide practical, hands-on guidance for home gardeners seeking to create habitat for local pollinators.

The trail opens in response to growing numbers of visitors who have asked the garden for more specific, actionable advice on how to landscape for pollinators. Garden staff observed that while general interest in pollinator conservation is high, many people want step-by-step demonstrations of which plants to choose and how to arrange them for maximum benefit. The Pollinator Path aims to bridge that gap by showcasing real-world installations that visitors can replicate at home.

“We hear from so many people who want to help, but they aren’t sure where to start,” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “The Pollinator Path shows that small, intentional planting decisions—like choosing a single native milkweed variety or adding a night-blooming flower near a porch light—can create connected patches of habitat that stretch across neighborhoods. You don’t need a vast property to make a difference.”

The trail features several distinct experiential stations. Native plant demonstration beds are planted with regionally appropriate species known to attract a range of pollinators, including showy goldenrod, purple coneflower, butterfly weed, and bee balm. Each bed is labeled with common and scientific names, bloom periods, and the specific pollinators each plant supports. A dedicated night-blooming flower station highlights species such as evening primrose and moonflower, which open at dusk to attract moths and night-flying insects.

To ensure accessibility for visitors with visual impairments, the trail incorporates tactile signage with raised text and embossed plant illustrations. Braille labels are installed at each station, and interpretive panels include high-contrast graphics and simple language suitable for all ages. The trail surface is paved and graded for wheelchair users and strollers, with shaded resting points placed at regular intervals along the loop.

Youth engagement is a core component of the trail experience. Harborleaf has produced reusable youth observation cards that children can pick up at the trailhead. The cards prompt young visitors to note specific details—such as the color of a flower, the number of bees on a bloom, or the shape of a moth’s wings—and include a simple checklist for identifying common pollinators. Garden educators hope the activity encourages families to spend more time observing and discussing the natural world.

On weekends, the garden will offer volunteer-led walks along the Pollinator Path. These free guided tours, led by trained docents, will cover topics such as seasonal bloom cycles, pollinator life cycles, and low-maintenance garden design strategies. The walks are scheduled for Saturday and Sunday mornings through the end of September, with additional evening walks during the peak night-blooming season.

The Pollinator Path is part of Harborleaf’s broader commitment to public environmental education. The garden provides year-round programming for school groups, community organizations, and individual visitors. According to garden leadership, the new trail aligns with a national trend of botanical gardens emphasizing applied ecology over purely ornamental displays.

“The response from our community has been tremendous,” Ortiz added. “We’ve seen that once people understand how a single garden bed connects to the next garden down the street, they become more invested in making their own yards welcoming to pollinators. That cumulative effect is powerful.”

The trail opening falls during peak summer bloom periods for many native plants, offering visitors immediate examples of pollinator activity. Harborleaf encourages visitors to bring water, sun protection, and comfortable walking shoes. The east meadow loop is open during regular garden hours, from 8 a.m. to sunset daily. Admission is included with general garden entry; no additional registration is required for self-guided visits. Weekend walks are open to the public on a first-come, first-served basis.

For more information about the Pollinator Path, including a downloadable trail map and a list of plant species featured on the demonstration beds, visit the garden’s website or contact the media office.

###

**Media Contact:**
Alex Chen
Communications Manager
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
(555) 123-4567
achen@harborleafgarden.org
www.harborleafgarden.org/pollinatorpath

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The blackout had swallowed the coastal strip whole. Inside the laundromat, the fluorescent hum died, leaving only the salt-scoured rattle of wind against the glass and the low, greenish glow of a single battery lantern set on a folding table. Two washers still churned, their cycles interrupted mid-swill, the sloshing sounds unnaturally loud in the sudden dark.

Elena stood with her hands on the edge of a dryer, her spine a question mark. She had been folding a single damp towel when the lights went, and now she held it, a blue cotton raft in the gloom. She hadn’t planned to be here. She’d driven two hours south to clear her head, ended up in this forgotten town of salt-grey shingles, and the laundromat was the only place open after nine. A mistake, clearly.

The door scraped open. A figure stepped in, backlit by a sliver of moon through the fog, then closed the door against the wind. He was tall, hunched slightly at the shoulders, and he carried a canvas duffel. He stopped when he saw her.

She knew him by the shape of his stillness first. The way he held himself, as if the world had shrunk to the size of a doorframe. Years drained away. “Nate?”

He blinked, the lantern light carving his face from shadow. Older. Thinner. A line of weariness between his brows. “Elena.” Not a question. He had known her too.

A long pause. The wind moaned through the eaves. One of the washers clicked, trying to restart.

“The power’s out for blocks,” he said, his voice rougher than she remembered. “Saw your car. Thought I’d check if someone was stranded.”

She almost smiled. He’d always been like that. The neighbor who noticed when your porch light was out. Who brought in your paper when you were sick. Then one Tuesday morning, he was gone. The rental house stood empty for three weeks before a college kid moved in. No note. No call. Just the echo of silence.

“I’m not stranded,” she said, folding the towel edge to edge. “Just… stuck with wet towels.”

He set his duffel down near the door, didn’t move closer. His hands hung loose at his sides. “That’s the last load before the blackout? They’ll be clammy by morning.”

“They’re just towels.” She draped the folded one over her arm. “I didn’t come here expecting hospitality.”

The corner of his mouth twitched. “You never did.”

Silence settled again, thicker than fog. Elena remembered the voicemail. It had come three days after he left, a message she had listened to exactly once, then saved and never deleted. She had deleted the rest of her inbox, cleared contacts, archived photos. But that one recording stayed, a ghost in the machine. *Hey. It’s Nate. I know this is strange, but I’m at the airport. I should have said something. I should have—* Then a long pause, breathing, a click. He never finished.

“Listen,” she said, her voice steadier than she felt. “I have three minutes left on that load, and then I’m going to stuff them wet in a bag and drive home. You don’t have to stay.”

He moved, finally. Not toward her, but to the folding table. He pulled out the chair—a cheap plastic thing—and sat. His hands rested on the table, palms up, as if offering something he didn’t have words for. “I can wait with you. If you want. Or not. I don’t want to make it worse.”

“Worse than what?”

“Than leaving without saying goodbye.”

The words fell between them like cold rain. Elena’s fingers tightened on the towel. She thought of the damp clothes in the washer, the half-finished cycle. Incomplete. Like their last conversation, the one that never happened. They had been friends, neighbors, leaning toward something softer. Then he vanished.

“Why didn’t you finish the message?” she asked. The question slipped out, unguarded, raw.

He looked at his hands. “Because I didn’t know how. I still don’t.”

The second washer clicked and stuttered. She walked over, pressed the lid, felt the wet weight inside. She could pull them now, wring them out, leave. But she didn’t. She turned back, her hip against the machine.

“I would have listened,” she said. “If you’d said it.”

“I know.” His voice was low, barely above the hum of the dying cycle. “That’s why I didn’t. A dead voicemail is easier to leave than a real conversation.”

She wanted to be angry. She had been angry, once. Stored it in her chest like a cold stone. But now, in the green lantern light, with the ocean gnawing at the shore beyond the glass, the stone had worn to something softer. Lunar and quiet.

“The towels,” she said, “will be fine. They’re just water.”

He looked at her. The lantern caught his eyes, deep-set, tired. “And us?”

She didn’t answer. She walked to the folding table and sat across from him, the stack of dry towels between them. She pulled a thin one from the pile and set the lantern on it, balancing the light. The shadow of his face softened.

“I don’t need an apology,” she said. “I think I just needed to know you remembered the phone number.”

“I memorized it,” he said. “When you moved in next door. I was twenty-two and terrified of you. You were so… certain.”

“I’m not certain about anything.” She laughed, a small, tired sound. “I came here because I couldn’t sleep. I drove two hours because the grocery store back home was out of vanilla extract.”

“Vanilla extract.”

“I’m not making it up.”

He smiled. A real one, faint, but there. After a moment he reached into his jacket and pulled out his phone. The screen glowed, low battery. He tapped it, turned it toward her. A voice memo app, one recording. The date: the week he left.

“I never deleted it,” he said. “I kept it because I didn’t know how to finish it. Still don’t.”

She looked at the file name: *Elena.old.* Three syllables. The weight of years.

“I still have yours too,” she said, hearing her own voice as if from a distance. “From the same day. You never left a callback number.”

He put the phone face-down on the table, between the lantern and the towels. His fingers rested near hers, not touching.

The last washer ended its cycle with a soft beep. The power did not return. Outside, the fog rolled in, muffling the world. The laundromat was a pocket of green light, two people, and the sound of wet cotton settling.

Elena did not reach for her bag. She let her hand stay where it was, palms flat on the table, her little finger a half-inch from his.

“I should probably let you finish your load,” he said. Not a dismissal. A question.

“It’s already done,” she said. “The machines stopped. But I’m not in a hurry.”

She stood. Gathered the wet towels. Draped them over her forearm, still damp, still unfinished. The voicemail sat between them, unsaid, unresolved.

He stood too. Not to stop her. To pick up the lantern.

“Take my side street out,” he said. “The main road is dark. I’ll hold this up until your taillights clear the bend.”

She nodded. Walked to the door. Looked back once.

He stood in the green light, one hand raised, the other holding the lantern high. An unfinished sentence, waiting for a second draft.

She stepped into the dark. The fog drank her shape. Behind her, the light held steady, a quiet star in the salt night, until the road curved and she could not see it anymore.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# Beyond the Blunt Hammer: Designing Rate Limits for Multi-Tenant APIs

**By: [Your Name]**

Every backend engineer eventually faces the same bitter lesson: an API without rate limiting is an API waiting to be accidentally (or maliciously) DoS’ed by its own customers. But when your API serves both a five-person startup and a Fortune 500 enterprise with dedicated integration teams, a one-size-fits-all rate limit is not just unfair—it’s dangerous. It punishes the loyal while failing to protect the infrastructure.

This article walks through the practical engineering decisions behind rate limiting for a multi-tenant API, focusing on the nuances that separate a working system from a production incident waiting to happen.

## 1. The Four Pillars: Authentication, Authorization, Quotas, and Rate Limits

Before designing anything, we must clarify the vocabulary. Confusing these four leads to implementation that feels wrong, scales poorly, or violates security.

- **Authentication** answers “Who are you?” It establishes identity via API keys, OAuth tokens, or mTLS certificates. Without it, you cannot apply any of the following.

- **Authorization** answers “What are you allowed to do?” It checks permissions: “Can this tenant write to the billing endpoint?” Rate limiting is not authorization. A tenant can be authorized to call an endpoint but still be rate-limited.

- **Quotas** are **long-term, cumulative limits** over a billing cycle: “You may make 100,000 API calls this month.” They are enforced at the end of the cycle (soft) or rejected mid-cycle (hard). Quotas are about *capacity planning and billing*.

- **Rate limits** are **short-term, sliding constraints** to protect infrastructure health: “You may make 100 requests per second.” They prevent a burst from overwhelming shared resources—databases, queues, workers.

**Key insight:** Quotas are a business contract. Rate limits are an operational safety net. A tenant that hits its quota should be told “upgrade your plan.” A tenant that hits a rate limit should be told “slow down—your burst is hurting the system.”

## 2. Algorithm Showdown: Token Bucket, Fixed Window, Sliding Window

Three algorithms dominate discussion. At a practical level, one is almost always the right choice for multi-tenant APIs.

### Fixed Window
Divide time into discrete windows (e.g., 1 second). Count requests in each window. Exceed the limit? Reject until the next window.

**Practical problem:** Burst at the boundary. A tenant sends 100 requests in the last millisecond of window N, then 100 requests in the first millisecond of window N+1. In 2 milliseconds, they made 200 requests—effectively double the intended rate. The system still sees 100 in each window. This defeats the purpose of rate limiting and creates unpredictable load.

### Sliding Window Log
Track a timestamp for each request. Count requests within the last, say, 60 seconds. This eliminates boundary bursts but requires O(n) storage per tenant. For thousands of tenants with high throughput, this becomes a memory and GC nightmare. It also introduces latency if the window is checked via a centralized store like Redis (SCAN + ZREMRANGEBYSCORE under load hurts).

### Token Bucket
Imagine a bucket that fills at a constant rate (refill rate) and holds a maximum number of tokens (burst capacity). Each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, the request is rejected. The bucket is *not* tied to a clock window—it’s a pure sliding mechanism.

**Why it wins for multi-tenant APIs:**
- **Allows short bursts** (the bucket can hold tokens for up to the burst capacity).
- **Smooths long-term rate** regardless of wall-clock windows.
- **Memory efficient:** One integer per tenant (current token count) + one timestamp (last refill time). You can even lazily calculate fills on access.
- **Fair under micro-bursts:** A tenant cannot exploit window edges because there are no windows.

**Trade-off:** Token bucket is not perfectly precise for “exactly N per hour” (that’s a quota). But for rate limiting, it is the gold standard. Implement it with a simple Lua script in Redis or a local in-memory structure per API instance (if you can tolerate eventual consistency across replicas).

## 3. Isolation: Don’t Punish an Entire Tenant for One Noisy Integration

Multi-tenant APIs often share a pool of workers. If you apply a single global rate limit per tenant, one misbehaving integration—say, a webhook client that loops on errors—can consume all of that tenant’s tokens, starving their legitimate users (e.g., their own customers calling your API via their app).

**Solution: Nested rate limit keys.**

Design a hierarchy:
1. **Tenant-level limit** (e.g., 1,000 req/s). This is the hard ceiling.
2. **Integration-level limit** (e.g., 100 req/s per API key or per client ID). This is the soft isolation.

When a request arrives, you check the integration-level bucket first. If it exceeds its sub-limit, reject immediately—that specific integration is blocked, but the tenant’s other integrations remain untouched. Only when a *global tenant burst* happens do you fall back to the tenant-level bucket.

**Practical implementation detail:** The integration-level limit should be stricter than the tenant-level, but they are not additive. If a tenant has 10 integrations each with 100 req/s, but the tenant cap is 500 req/s, the effective limit is min(integration sum, tenant cap). This prevents a tenant from increasing their limit simply by creating more API keys.

## 4. Headers and Error Messages: The Client Needs to Self-Heal

Rate limiting is only useful if clients can respond intelligently. Return these headers on every response, whether successful or rejected:

- `X-RateLimit-Limit`: The maximum requests allowed in the current rate limit window (or per second for token bucket). Tell the client the ceiling.
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining`: How many tokens remain in the bucket at the moment the response was sent. Use this with care—it’s a snapshot and can change instantly.
- `X-RateLimit-Reset`: For token bucket, this is problematic because there is no fixed reset time. Instead, return `Retry-After` in seconds for *throttled* responses. This is the single most important header for self-healing clients.

**Error responses:**

For 429 Too Many Requests:
```json
{
  "error": "rate_limit_exceeded",
  "message": "You have exceeded the rate limit for this endpoint. Please reduce your request rate and retry after the specified Retry-After period.",
  "retry_after_seconds": 30
}
```

Crucially, *differentiate from quota violations*. Do not return 429 for quota errors. Use 403 Forbidden with `error: "quota_exceeded"` and a link to the billing portal. This avoids client confusion and support tickets.

## 5. Observability: Do the Limits Actually Work?

A rate limiter that never triggers is either too generous or dead code. A rate limiter that triggers constantly is blocking legitimate use. Observability must answer both sides.

### Metrics (every 15s or via histogram)
- `rate_limit.hits`: Total requests evaluated.
- `rate_limit.throttled`: Count of 429 responses, broken down by `tenant_id`, `integration_id`, and `endpoint`.
- `rate_limit.token_bucket_usage_pct`: Average fill level of the token bucket per tenant. If this stays near 100% for a premium tenant during normal load, your limits might be too low.
- `rate_limit.retry_after_seconds`: Distribution of wait times. If most are < 1s, clients can retry immediately—fine. If median is > 30s, you’re causing severe degradation.

### Logs
Log every throttled request with:
- `tenant_id`, `integration_id`, `endpoint`, `method`.
- `context`: Was this a tenant-level or integration-level rejection?
- `current_bucket_level`: Helps debugging false positives.

Do **not** log every successful request—that’s a firehose. Use sampled logging for success paths.

### Alerts
- **High throttle rate for a single tenant:** If one tenant is being throttled >5% of their requests, alert. That tenant has a noisy integration or misconfigured client.
- **Global throttle rate rising:** If overall 429 rate exceeds 1% of traffic, investigate load drivers. Are your limits too low, or is there an attack?
- **Bucket depletion for critical tenants:** If a VIP enterprise tenant’s token bucket stays near zero during their normal business hours, your limits are too tight. Alert the engineering team, not the tenant.

## 6. Recommended Baseline Design

For a generic multi-tenant API serving 10 to 10,000 tenants:

- **Algorithm:** Token bucket. Redis-backed for distributed consistency (one key per tenant, one key per integration). Lua script for atomic check-and-consume. TTL on keys to auto-clean idle tenants.
- **Default limits:** 100 req/s per tenant (burst 200); 20 req/s per integration (burst 40). Adjust via per-tenant configuration in a database table that feeds a hot reload cache (e.g., a 30-second TTL Redis hash).
- **Key hierarchy:** `rate_limit:{tenant_id}:global` → `rate_limit:{tenant_id}:integration:{integration_id}`.
- **Headers:** Every response includes `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `Retry-After` on 429.
- **Observability:** StatsD or Prometheus counters with tenant/integration labels. Alerts on >5% throttling for any tenant with >1,000 requests/minute.
- **Fallback:** If Redis is down, fall back to an in-memory token bucket with a per-instance rate limit *higher* than normal (and log loudly). Do not block traffic on a rate limiter outage.

Rate limiting is not about punishing users—it’s about protecting the system so that users can keep using it. When designed with isolation, clear signals, and observability, it becomes invisible infrastructure. When done poorly, it becomes a support ticket factory. Choose token bucket, isolate integrations, and measure everything. Your tenants—and your on-call engineer—will thank you.

messages.content:
请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters should never have been written in the first place. And among those that are written, a considerable number are so incomplete in information or disorderly in structure that what could have been settled in two letters drags into six or eight rounds of correspondence. Good letters come from clear thinking and careful planning. A sales letter may require weeks of refinement; a routine business reply may take only a few minutes. What matters is not how long it took, but whether the final letter accomplishes its task accurately.

There are many rules for measuring a business letter, but only two are truly indispensable: first, the letter must make your meaning clear to the recipient; second, it must build goodwill for the company. The best letters achieve both objectives in the most concise, courteous, and complete manner possible. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it — even if it is merely thanking a client. Too much effort is wasted on aimless advertisements, ineffective solicitations, and unnecessary correspondence; these texts consume typewriters and paper without moving anything forward.

A good letter should not draw attention to its own style, but should allow the reader to receive the message it conveys. A publishing house once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Recipients praised the letters for their elegance, yet no one placed an order. This shows that when the writing overshadows the message, no matter how graceful it may be, it is not successful business writing. Letterhead, layout, and paragraph structure should be neat, restrained, and clear; overly flashy designs or contrived formatting only distract.

Business writing should also respect the reader’s time. Clear subject lines, appropriate salutations, paragraphs organized by topic, specific requests, and complete contact details build trust far more effectively than ornate phrasing. If a letter requires a reply, the writer should indicate by when they expect a response, what information is needed, and what the next steps will be. In this way, the letter not only conveys an attitude but also moves the matter forward.

请将下面的繁体中文寓言/哲理片段翻译成英文，目标读者为青少年读者。要求：保留想象力和节奏；遇到典故不要长篇解释；译文应清楚、流畅，适合作为儿童文学或寓言文本阅读。

素材来源：《莊子的故事》节选：逍遥游开篇
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23913
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
**The Tale of Kun and Peng**  

In the Northern Ocean, there lived a fish named Kun. How big was Kun? It stretched for thousands of miles—no one knew its true size. Then it transformed into a bird named Peng. How vast was Peng’s back? It, too, spanned thousands of miles. When it beat its wings in fury, they hung like clouds draping the sky.  

When the sea stirred, this bird would journey to the Southern Ocean—the Heavenly Pool.  

A curious book called *Qi Xie* records strange tales. It says: “When Peng flies to the Southern Ocean, it churns the water for three thousand miles. Then, riding a whirlwind, it soars ninety thousand miles high, leaving only after the sixth-month gale.”  

How wild the dance of dust and sunbeams! Are these living things, too, riding the breath of the world?  

The sky—is it truly blue, or just the distant echo of endlessness? From high above, does Peng see our world the same way we see the blue?  

If water is too shallow, it cannot carry a great boat. Pour a cup of water in a hollow on the floor; a blade of grass becomes a boat. Put a cup there, and it sticks—the water is too shallow, the cup too big. So it is with wind. If it is not deep enough, it cannot bear a great bird’s wings. That is why Peng must rise ninety thousand miles—so the wind is beneath it, strong and steady. Only then, with the blue sky on its back, can it turn southward and begin its journey.  

A cicada and a little dove laughed, saying: “We fly with all our might, but we stop at the nearest tree. Sometimes we don’t even make it; we just crash to the ground. Why fly ninety thousand miles and go south?”  

If you’re just going to the woods, you take three meals and come back full. If you’re going a hundred miles, you grind grain the night before. If you’re going a thousand miles, you prepare food for three months. What do those two little creatures know?  

Small knowledge cannot match great knowledge. Short-lived cannot fathom the long-lived. How do we know? A morning mushroom never sees the full moon. A summer cicada never knows winter or spring. That is a short life.  

To the south of Chu, there is a magic tree. Five hundred years is its spring, five hundred years its autumn. In ancient times, there was a camphor tree that had eight thousand years of spring and eight thousand years of autumn. That is a long life.  

Yet people look at old Peng Zu, who lived long, and want to measure themselves against him. Is that not sad?  

Emperor Tang once asked Ji, the wise man, about this. In the barren north, there is a vast sea—the Heavenly Pool. In it lives a fish thousands of miles wide. No one knows its length. Its name is Kun. There is also a bird named Peng, with a back like Mount Tai and wings like clouds hanging from the sky. It rides the whirlwind, soaring ninety thousand miles, piercing the clouds, carrying the blue sky. Then it heads south toward the Southern Ocean.  

A tiny quail laughed at it, saying: “Where does he think he’s going? I leap and fly, just a few yards high, then drop among the weeds. That’s the best kind of flying! Where on earth does he think he’s going?”  

That is the difference between small and great.  

So, those whose talent fits a small office, whose deeds suit one town, whose virtue pleases one prince, whose name is known in one kingdom—they see themselves as that quail did. But Song Rongzi laughed at them.  

The whole world could praise him, and he wouldn’t work harder. The whole world could criticize him, and he wouldn’t feel down. He knew clearly what was inside and outside, and where honor and shame truly lie. That was enough. Still, even he hadn’t reached the highest point.  

Liezi could ride the wind, light and graceful, and return after fifteen days. He was free from walking, but he still depended on something.  

But one who rides the true nature of heaven and earth, who masters the six seasons of change, and travels through the endless—what does he need to depend on?  

Therefore, the Perfect One has no self. The Divine One has no merit. The Sage has no name.
