Let me see: that would be four thousand miles down, I think—” (for, you see, Alice had learnt several things of this sort in her lessons in the schoolroom, and though this was not a _very_ good opportunity for showing off her knowledge, as there was no one to listen to her, still it was good practice to say it over) “—yes, that’s about the right distance—but then I wonder what Latitude or Longitude I’ve got to?” (Alice had no idea what Latitude was, or Longitude either, but thought they were nice grand words to say.)

message.content:
爱丽丝挨着姐姐坐在河岸上，实在觉得无聊透顶：姐姐读的那本书既没有插图，也没有对话，"一本书要是连图画和对话都没有，"爱丽丝想，"那还有什么意思呢？"

她暗自琢磨着（可大热天让她昏昏沉沉的，怎么也打不起精神来），究竟是编个雏菊花环好玩呢，还是值得费劲儿起身去摘雏菊。就在这时，一只长着粉红眼睛的白兔突然从她身边跑过。

这件事本身倒没什么稀奇的；爱丽丝听见兔子自言自语"哎呀！哎呀！我可要迟到了！"时，也没觉得有什么不对劲（事后回想起来，她才觉得这事本该让她大吃一惊的，可当时一切瞧着都那么自然）；可当这只兔子真的从背心口袋里掏出一块怀表，看了看，又急匆匆往前赶时，爱丽丝一下子跳了起来——她脑子里闪过一个念头：自己从没见过兔子穿背心，更没见过兔子从口袋里掏表！她满心好奇，飞奔过田野追了上去。幸好，她刚好看见兔子一头钻进了树篱底下的大兔洞里。

眨眼功夫，爱丽丝也跟着跳了进去，压根儿没想过自己到底该怎么出来。

兔洞起初笔直向前，像条隧道，可突然猛地向下倾斜——陡得让爱丽丝来不及站住脚，就发现自己掉进了一口深井里。

要么是井太深，要么是她落得太慢，反正往下坠的时候有足够的时间东张西望，琢磨接下来会发生什么。她先低头想看看自己会掉到什么地方，可底下黑漆漆的什么也看不见；接着她打量井壁，发现四周全是碗橱和书架，这儿那儿还挂着钉在木桩上的地图和图画。她经过一个架子时顺手拿下一个罐子，上面贴着"橘子酱"的标签——可让她大失所望的是，罐子是空的。她不敢把罐子扔下去，怕砸着底下的人，于是趁经过另一个碗橱时，设法把它塞了进去。

"好哇！"爱丽丝心想，"经过这么一摔，以后从楼梯上滚下去我根本不当回事啦！家里人会多佩服我呀！哼，就算从房顶上摔下来，我也一个字都不会提！"（这话倒很可能是真的。）

往下，往下，往下。这坠落难道永远没个尽头？"我猜猜，这会儿掉下去多少英里了？"她大声说，"我准是快到地心了吧。算算看——大概有四千英里深——"（要知道，爱丽丝在课堂上可学过不少这类知识，虽说眼下没人听她显摆，算不上什么好机会，可嘴里念叨念叨总归是个练习）"——嗯，差不多就是这个距离——可这会儿我到了什么纬度或经度了呢？"（爱丽丝根本不知道纬度或经度是什么，只是觉得这些词儿说起来又响亮又神气。）

messages.content:
Translate the following English news-release excerpt into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: accurate, natural, suitable for a general news audience; keep the institutional name as “美国地质调查局（USGS）” on first mention; do not add facts or commentary.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey news release: New USGS diagram re-envisions how Earth’s water cycles the planet
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
RESTON, Va.— Starting today, educators around the nation will have a more accurate and more comprehensive tool to explain the Earth’s water cycle with the unveiling of the new U.S. Geological Survey water cycle diagram.

The revised version replaces one used by hundreds of thousands of educators and students internationally every year since 2000. So why the new water cycle? This depiction brings humans into the picture, showing the water cycle as a complex interplay of small, interconnected cycles that people interact with and influence, rather than one big circle.

USGS experts consulted with more than 100 educators and more than 30 hydrologic experts to develop the new diagram. The vast amounts of water data that USGS has collected in recent decades has informed a nuanced perspective of the water cycle, demonstrating how both its human and natural components are interconnected.

Where the existing water cycle diagram depicted only the natural aspects of the cycle, the new version depicts how Earth’s water moves and is stored, both naturally and because of human actions. Not only does the new diagram illustrate a more comprehensive view of the water cycle, it draws on principles of information design to focus attention on the water as it moves through the natural and built environment. It shows how multiple ecosystems—including a coastal plain, dry basin, wet basin, and agricultural basin—are connected across watersheds and at continental scales.

The new diagram will initially be available in both English and Spanish, with the expectation it will be translated into many other languages by end users, as was the previous version.

message.content:
弗吉尼亚州雷斯顿讯——自今日起，全美教育工作者将拥有一个更准确、更全面的工具来解释地球水循环，因为美国地质调查局（USGS）发布了全新的水循环示意图。

此次更新的版本取代了自2000年以来每年被全球数十万教育工作者和学生使用的旧版示意图。为何要推出新的水循环图？因为新图将人类活动纳入其中，展示水循环并非一个单一的循环圈，而是由多个小型、相互关联的循环组成的复杂互动体系，人类既参与其中，也对其产生影响。

美国地质调查局（USGS）的专家咨询了100多名教育工作者和30多位水文专家，共同开发了这张新示意图。美国地质调查局（USGS）近几十年来收集的大量水文数据，为其提供了对水循环的细致视角，揭示了水循环中人类与自然组成部分之间的相互联系。

旧版水循环示意图仅描绘了水循环的自然方面，而新版本则展示了地球上的水如何因自然作用和人类活动而流动和储存。新示意图不仅呈现了对水循环更全面的认识，还借鉴了信息设计原理，将注意力聚焦于水在自然环境和人造环境中的运动过程。图中展示了多个生态系统——包括沿海平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地和农业盆地——如何跨越流域、在乃至大陆尺度上相互连接。

新版示意图将首先提供英语和西班牙语版本，预计最终用户将像翻译旧版一样，将其翻译成多种其他语言。

messages.content:
Translate the following English literary passage into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve emotional tension, social nuance, and indirect narration; do not make the style slangy; names may be translated conventionally or kept in English consistently. Do not add explanations.

素材来源：Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice, Chapter XXXIV excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1342
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When they were gone, Elizabeth, as if intending to exasperate herself as much as possible against Mr. Darcy, chose for her employment the examination of all the letters which Jane had written to her since her being in Kent. They contained no actual complaint, nor was there any revival of past occurrences, or any communication of present suffering. But in all, and in almost every line of each, there was a want of that cheerfulness which had been used to characterize her style, and which, proceeding from the serenity of a mind at ease with itself, and kindly disposed towards everyone, had been scarcely ever clouded. Elizabeth noticed every sentence conveying the idea of uneasiness, with an attention which it had hardly received on the first perusal. Mr. Darcy’s shameful boast of what misery he had been able to inflict gave her a keener sense of her sister’s sufferings. It was some consolation to think that his visit to Rosings was to end on the day after the next, and a still greater that in less than a fortnight she should herself be with Jane again, and enabled to contribute to the recovery of her spirits, by all that affection could do.

She could not think of Darcy’s leaving Kent without remembering that his cousin was to go with him; but Colonel Fitzwilliam had made it clear that he had no intentions at all, and, agreeable as he was, she did not mean to be unhappy about him.

While settling this point, she was suddenly roused by the sound of the door-bell; and her spirits were a little fluttered by the idea of its being Colonel Fitzwilliam himself, who had once before called late in the evening, and might now come to inquire particularly after her. But this idea was soon banished, and her spirits were very differently affected, when, to her utter amazement, she saw Mr. Darcy walk into the room. In a hurried manner he immediately began an inquiry after her health, imputing his visit to a wish of hearing that she were better. She answered him with cold civility. He sat down for a few moments, and then getting up walked about the room. Elizabeth was surprised, but said not a word. After a silence of several minutes, he came towards her in an agitated manner, and thus began:--

“In vain have I struggled. It will not do. My feelings will not be repressed. You must allow me to tell you how ardently I admire and love you.”

Elizabeth’s astonishment was beyond expression. She stared, coloured, doubted, and was silent. This he considered sufficient encouragement, and the avowal of all that he felt and had long felt for her immediately followed. He spoke well; but there were feelings besides those of the heart to be detailed, and he was not more eloquent on the subject of tenderness than of pride. His sense of her inferiority, of its being a degradation, of the family obstacles which judgment had always opposed to inclination, were dwelt on with a warmth which seemed due to the consequence he was wounding, but was very unlikely to recommend his suit.

In spite of her deeply-rooted dislike, she could not be insensible to the compliment of such a man’s affection, and though her intentions did not vary for an instant, she was at first sorry for the pain he was to receive; till roused to resentment by his subsequent language, she lost all compassion in anger. She tried, however, to compose herself to answer him with patience, when he should have done. He concluded with representing to her the strength of that attachment which in spite of all his endeavours he had found impossible to conquer; and with expressing his hope that it would now be rewarded by her acceptance of his hand. As he said this she could easily see that he had no doubt of a favourable answer. He _spoke_ of apprehension and anxiety, but his countenance expressed real security. Such a circumstance could only exasperate farther; and when he ceased the colour rose into her cheeks and she said,--

“In such cases as this, it is, I believe, the established mode to express a sense of obligation for the sentiments avowed, however unequally they may be returned. It is natural that obligation should be felt, and if I could _feel_ gratitude, I would now thank you. But I cannot--I have never desired your good opinion, and you have certainly bestowed it most unwillingly. I am sorry to have occasioned pain to anyone. It has been most unconsciously done, however, and I hope will be of short duration. The feelings which you tell me have long prevented the acknowledgment of your regard can have little difficulty in overcoming it after this explanation.”

message.content:
两人走后，伊丽莎白像是存心要让自己对达西先生更加愤恨，便挑出简自到肯特以来写给她的所有信件，逐一细读。信中并无实际抱怨之词，也未重提旧事，更未倾诉眼前苦楚。然而每封信的字里行间，都少了她平素行文的那份欢快——那份源自心平气和、与世无争、善待众人的恬淡心境，原本几乎从未蒙上过阴霾。伊丽莎白留意着每一句流露不安的话语，其专注程度远甚于初读之时。达西先生对自己造成的痛苦竟引以为耻地自鸣得意，这令她更真切地感受到姐姐所受的煎熬。想到他后天便要结束在罗辛斯的拜访，多少是个安慰；而更大的慰藉是，不出半月自己便能回到简身边，用所有的温情帮助她恢复心绪。

想到达西即将离开肯特，她不免忆起他的表兄也将同去；但费茨威廉上校早已表明他毫无此意，况且此人虽可亲，她也不打算为他伤神。

正思忖间，门铃声骤起，她蓦地一惊。旋即想到或许是费茨威廉上校本人——他曾有一次深夜来访，此番也许又是专程来问候她。这念头转瞬即逝，而待到看清走进房间的竟是达西先生时，她的心绪全然换了滋味。他匆匆开口问安，声称来访是盼知她身体已见好转。她以冷淡的礼数回应。他坐了片刻，又起身在房里踱步。伊丽莎白甚是诧异，却一言不发。沉默了数分钟后，他激动地走到她面前，开口道：

“我挣扎了许久，终究无用。克制不住了。我的情感无法再压抑。请允许我告诉你，我多么热烈地倾慕你、爱你。”

伊丽莎白惊诧得无以复加。她瞪着眼，红了脸，满腹狐疑，默不作声。他视此为足够的鼓励，当即倾吐了压抑已久的全部爱意。他言辞流畅，但除了心迹之外，还需坦白其他情绪——他谈起柔情时，并不比谈到骄傲时更有口才。他提到她的出身低微，觉得这是自降身份，提到那些理智始终在抗拒感情的家族障碍——这些话说得慷慨激昂，仿佛为的是对得起他此刻正在伤害的门第尊严，却丝毫不能为其求婚增色。

尽管她对他厌恶根深蒂固，但面对这样一个男人的爱慕，她也无法全然漠视这番恭维。尽管她的心意分毫未改，起初仍为他即将承受的痛苦感到些许歉疚；及至被他接下去的话语激怒，那份怜悯便尽数化为恼怒。然而她还是竭力镇定下来，准备等他讲完后再以耐心作答。他最后向她表明，这份情意他无论怎样努力也终究无法战胜；并希望如今能得到回报——接受他的求婚。他说这话时，她轻易看出他对肯定的答复深信不疑。他嘴上说着担忧与焦虑，脸上却透着十足的把握。这情形只能更加激怒她。待他说完，她双颊绯红，开口道：

“在诸如此类的情形下，我相信通常的做法是：对于对方表露的心迹，无论回应之情多么不对等，也该表示一番感激。感激之情自然是难免的，倘若我心中确有谢意，此刻便会向你道谢。但我不能——我从不曾希求你的垂青，而你给予这份垂青也极不情愿。我不愿给任何人造成痛苦，不过此事纯属无心之失，但愿这痛苦不会长久。你方才告诉我，你长期以来的情感曾阻挠你承认这份心意——经过此番解释，克服这点障碍想来不难。”

messages.content:
Translate the following technical explainer into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve the technical meaning, use clear science-education language, and keep terms such as evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, groundwater recharge, and aquifer accurate. Do not add external information.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
水循环描述了水在地球上的分布及其运动方式。人类用水、土地利用和气候变化都会影响水循环。通过理解这些影响，我们能够朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

从太空俯瞰，我们星球最引人注目的特征便是水。无论是液态还是固态，水覆盖了地球表面75%的面积，并在天空中汇聚成云。水几乎遍布地球各处——从地壳岩石内部到人体细胞之中。需要牢记的是，所有这些水都在我们星球上持续运动着。

水储存在大气中、地表和地下。它可以呈现液态、固态或气态。液态水可以是淡水或咸水。水在其储存场所之间移动。水在大尺度上运动，流经流域、大气层和地下深处；也在极小的尺度上运动，存在于我们体内、植物和其他生物体中。

海洋储存了地球上96%的水。海水是咸水，即含有盐分。在陆地上，咸水储存在咸水湖中。地球上其余的水是淡水。淡水以液态形式储存在淡水湖、人工水库、河流和湿地中。水以固态形式储存在冰盖、冰川以及高海拔或靠近地球两极的积雪中。

在运动过程中，水可以在液态、固态和气态之间转换。洋流混合了海洋中的水，大气环流则输送水蒸气。水通过蒸发、蒸散发和降水在大气与地表之间运动。水通过融雪、径流和河川径流在地表运动。水通过入渗和地下水补给进入地下。在地下，地下水在含水层内流动。地下水可通过自然排泄方式（如流入河流、海洋或从泉水中涌出）返回地表。

水既会自然运动，也会因人类活动而运动。太阳能量和重力驱动着水在各储水库之间持续运动。太阳能量使液态水蒸发为水蒸气。蒸散发是水从地表和海洋进入大气的主要途径。重力使水在陆地上向下流动，也导致雨、雪和冰雹从云层中降落。

人类和其他生物依赖水生存。可用水量取决于每个储水库中的水量、水在水循环中运动的时间和速率，以及水的洁净程度。通过理解人类活动对水循环的影响，我们能够朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

messages.content:
Write a single-file Python 3 command-line program named `schedule_optimizer.py`. Use no third-party packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Problem: Given a list of candidate work sessions, choose a non-overlapping subset that maximizes total value. Each session has an ID, start time, end time, and value. This is weighted interval scheduling with reconstruction.

Input: read JSON from standard input. Format:
{
  "sessions": [
    {"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:30:00", "value": 5}
  ]
}

Requirements:
1. Parse ISO-like timestamps without time zones using Python standard library.
2. Reject sessions whose end is not after start or whose value is negative.
3. Output JSON with `max_value`, `selected_ids` in chronological order, and `rejected` entries with reasons.
4. If multiple optimal solutions exist, choose the one with fewer sessions; if still tied, choose lexicographically smaller list of IDs.
5. Include an efficient O(n log n) dynamic programming solution.
6. Include built-in tests. When run with `--test`, execute tests and print a short success message. Normal mode should print only JSON.

Output only the complete Python code. Do not include explanations.

message.content:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
import json
from datetime import datetime

def parse_timestamp(ts_str):
    """Parse ISO-like timestamp without timezone info."""
    # Remove 'Z' if present and handle microseconds
    if ts_str.endswith('Z'):
        ts_str = ts_str[:-1]
    return datetime.fromisoformat(ts_str)

def validate_sessions(sessions):
    """Validate sessions and return (valid_sessions, rejected_list)."""
    valid = []
    rejected = []
    for session in sessions:
        sid = session.get('id', 'unknown')
        reasons = []
        
        # Check required fields
        if 'start' not in session or 'end' not in session:
            reasons.append("missing start or end")
            rejected.append({"id": sid, "reasons": reasons})
            continue
        
        # Parse timestamps
        try:
            start = parse_timestamp(session['start'])
            end = parse_timestamp(session['end'])
        except (ValueError, TypeError):
            reasons.append("invalid timestamp format")
            rejected.append({"id": sid, "reasons": reasons})
            continue
        
        # Validate order
        if end <= start:
            reasons.append("end not after start")
        
        # Validate value
        value = session.get('value', 0)
        if not isinstance(value, (int, float)) or value < 0:
            reasons.append("negative or invalid value")
        
        if reasons:
            rejected.append({"id": sid, "reasons": reasons})
        else:
            # Use original session dict with parsed timestamps
            valid.append({
                'id': sid,
                'start': start,
                'end': end,
                'value': value,
                'original': session
            })
    
    return valid, rejected

def weighted_interval_scheduling(sessions):
    """
    O(n log n) weighted interval scheduling with tie-breaking:
    - maximize value
    - if tie, fewer sessions
    - if still tie, lexicographically smaller list of IDs
    """
    if not sessions:
        return 0, [], []
    
    # Sort by end time
    sorted_sessions = sorted(sessions, key=lambda s: s['end'])
    ids = [s['id'] for s in sorted_sessions]
    starts = [s['start'] for s in sorted_sessions]
    ends = [s['end'] for s in sorted_sessions]
    values = [s['value'] for s in sorted_sessions]
    n = len(sorted_sessions)
    
    # Binary search for last non-overlapping session
    def find_last_non_overlapping(i):
        """Find rightmost session that ends before session i starts."""
        start_time = starts[i]
        low, high = 0, i - 1
        result = -1
        while low <= high:
            mid = (low + high) // 2
            if ends[mid] <= start_time:
                result = mid
                low = mid + 1
            else:
                high = mid - 1
        return result
    
    # DP arrays: dp_value[i] = max value up to i, dp_sessions[i] = list of indices
    dp_value = [0] * n
    dp_sessions = [[] for _ in range(n)]  # store session indices for solution
    
    prev = [find_last_non_overlapping(i) for i in range(n)]
    
    for i in range(n):
        # Option 1: exclude current session
        exclude_value = dp_value[i-1] if i > 0 else 0
        exclude_sessions = dp_sessions[i-1][:] if i > 0 else []
        
        # Option 2: include current session
        include_value = values[i]
        include_sessions = [i]
        if prev[i] != -1:
            include_value += dp_value[prev[i]]
            include_sessions = dp_sessions[prev[i]][:] + [i]
        
        # Compare: prefer higher value
        if include_value > exclude_value:
            dp_value[i] = include_value
            dp_sessions[i] = include_sessions
        elif include_value < exclude_value:
            dp_value[i] = exclude_value
            dp_sessions[i] = exclude_sessions
        else:
            # Tie in value: choose fewer sessions
            if len(include_sessions) < len(exclude_sessions):
                dp_value[i] = include_value
                dp_sessions[i] = include_sessions
            elif len(include_sessions) > len(exclude_sessions):
                dp_value[i] = exclude_value
                dp_sessions[i] = exclude_sessions
            else:
                # Same number of sessions: lexicographically smaller IDs
                include_ids = [ids[idx] for idx in include_sessions]
                exclude_ids = [ids[idx] for idx in exclude_sessions]
                if include_ids < exclude_ids:
                    dp_value[i] = include_value
                    dp_sessions[i] = include_sessions
                else:
                    dp_value[i] = exclude_value
                    dp_sessions[i] = exclude_sessions
    
    max_value = dp_value[-1]
    selected_indices = dp_sessions[-1]
    selected_ids = [ids[idx] for idx in selected_indices]
    # Selected sessions in chronological order (already sorted by end time)
    selected_sessions = [sorted_sessions[idx] for idx in selected_indices]
    
    return max_value, selected_ids, selected_sessions

def run_test():
    """Run built-in tests."""
    tests_passed = 0
    total_tests = 0
    
    # Test 1: Basic example
    total_tests += 1
    input_data = {
        "sessions": [
            {"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:30:00", "value": 5},
            {"id": "b", "start": "2026-05-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T11:00:00", "value": 3},
            {"id": "c", "start": "2026-05-01T11:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T12:00:00", "value": 4}
        ]
    }
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions(input_data['sessions'])
    max_val, sel_ids, _ = weighted_interval_scheduling(valid)
    assert max_val == 9, f"Test 1 failed: expected 9, got {max_val}"
    assert sel_ids == ['a', 'c'], f"Test 1 failed: expected ['a','c'], got {sel_ids}"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    # Test 2: Empty input
    total_tests += 1
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions([])
    max_val, sel_ids, _ = weighted_interval_scheduling(valid)
    assert max_val == 0 and sel_ids == [], "Test 2 failed"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    # Test 3: Overlapping sessions - pick highest value
    total_tests += 1
    input_data = {
        "sessions": [
            {"id": "x", "start": "2026-01-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "value": 10},
            {"id": "y", "start": "2026-01-01T10:30:00", "end": "2026-01-01T11:30:00", "value": 8},
            {"id": "z", "start": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T12:00:00", "value": 7}
        ]
    }
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions(input_data['sessions'])
    max_val, sel_ids, _ = weighted_interval_scheduling(valid)
    assert max_val == 17, f"Test 3 failed: expected 17, got {max_val}"
    assert sel_ids == ['x', 'z'], f"Test 3 failed: expected ['x','z'], got {sel_ids}"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    # Test 4: Invalid sessions
    total_tests += 1
    input_data = {
        "sessions": [
            {"id": "bad1", "start": "2026-01-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T09:00:00", "value": 5},
            {"id": "bad2", "start": "2026-01-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "value": -1},
            {"id": "good", "start": "2026-01-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "value": 5}
        ]
    }
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions(input_data['sessions'])
    assert len(rejected) == 2, f"Test 4 failed: expected 2 rejected, got {len(rejected)}"
    assert len(valid) == 1, f"Test 4 failed: expected 1 valid, got {len(valid)}"
    max_val, sel_ids, _ = weighted_interval_scheduling(valid)
    assert max_val == 5 and sel_ids == ['good'], "Test 4 failed"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    # Test 5: Tie-breaking - fewer sessions
    total_tests += 1
    input_data = {
        "sessions": [
            {"id": "a", "start": "2026-01-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T12:00:00", "value": 10},
            {"id": "b", "start": "2026-01-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "value": 5},
            {"id": "c", "start": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T12:00:00", "value": 5}
        ]
    }
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions(input_data['sessions'])
    max_val, sel_ids, _ = weighted_interval_scheduling(valid)
    assert max_val == 10, f"Test 5 failed: expected 10, got {max_val}"
    assert sel_ids == ['a'], f"Test 5 failed: expected ['a'], got {sel_ids}"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    # Test 6: Tie-breaking - lexicographically smaller
    total_tests += 1
    input_data = {
        "sessions": [
            {"id": "b", "start": "2026-01-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "value": 5},
            {"id": "c", "start": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T12:00:00", "value": 5},
            {"id": "a", "start": "2026-01-01T10:30:00", "end": "2026-01-01T12:30:00", "value": 10}
        ]
    }
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions(input_data['sessions'])
    # Two solutions: ['a']=10, ['b','c']=10 -> fewer sessions wins (['a'])
    max_val, sel_ids, _ = weighted_interval_scheduling(valid)
    assert max_val == 10, f"Test 6 failed: expected 10, got {max_val}"
    assert sel_ids == ['a'], f"Test 6 failed: expected ['a'], got {sel_ids}"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    # Test 7: Multiple optimal with same count - lexicographic
    total_tests += 1
    input_data = {
        "sessions": [
            {"id": "z", "start": "2026-01-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "value": 5},
            {"id": "a", "start": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "end": "2026-01-01T12:00:00", "value": 5}
        ]
    }
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions(input_data['sessions'])
    max_val, sel_ids, _ = weighted_interval_scheduling(valid)
    assert max_val == 10, f"Test 7 failed: expected 10, got {max_val}"
    assert sel_ids == ['a', 'z'], f"Test 7 failed: expected ['a','z'], got {sel_ids}"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    # Test 8: Invalid timestamp format
    total_tests += 1
    input_data = {
        "sessions": [
            {"id": "badts", "start": "not-a-date", "end": "2026-01-01T11:00:00", "value": 5}
        ]
    }
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions(input_data['sessions'])
    assert len(rejected) == 1, "Test 8 failed"
    assert 'invalid timestamp format' in rejected[0]['reasons'], "Test 8 failed"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    # Test 9: Z suffix handling
    total_tests += 1
    input_data = {
        "sessions": [
            {"id": "ztest", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00Z", "end": "2026-05-01T10:30:00Z", "value": 5}
        ]
    }
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions(input_data['sessions'])
    assert len(valid) == 1, "Test 9 failed"
    max_val, sel_ids, _ = weighted_interval_scheduling(valid)
    assert max_val == 5 and sel_ids == ['ztest'], "Test 9 failed"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    # Test 10: Missing fields
    total_tests += 1
    input_data = {
        "sessions": [
            {"id": "missing", "value": 5}
        ]
    }
    valid, rejected = validate_sessions(input_data['sessions'])
    assert len(rejected) == 1, "Test 10 failed"
    tests_passed += 1
    
    print(f"All {tests_passed}/{total_tests} tests passed!")

def main():
    if len(sys.argv) > 1 and sys.argv[1] == '--test':
        run_test()
        return
    
    try:
        raw_input = sys.stdin.read()
        if not raw_input.strip():
            output = {"max_value": 0, "selected_ids": [], "rejected": []}
            print(json.dumps(output))
            return
        
        data = json.loads(raw_input)
        sessions = data.get('sessions', [])
        
        valid_sessions, rejected = validate_sessions(sessions)
        
        max_value, selected_ids, _ = weighted_interval_scheduling(valid_sessions)
        
        output = {
            "max_value": max_value,
            "selected_ids": selected_ids,
            "rejected": rejected
        }
        print(json.dumps(output))
    
    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
        output = {
            "max_value": 0,
            "selected_ids": [],
            "rejected": [{"id": "input", "reasons": [f"Invalid JSON: {str(e)}"]}]
        }
        print(json.dumps(output))
        sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

messages.content:
Write a single-file Node.js HTTP server named `server.js`. Use only built-in Node.js modules; no Express, no npm packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Task: implement a small JSON API for a help-desk ticket queue stored in a local JSON file.

Data model:
- Ticket: `id`, `subject`, `description`, `priority` (`low`, `medium`, `high`), `status` (`open`, `in_progress`, `resolved`), `created_at`, `updated_at`.

API requirements:
1. `GET /tickets` with optional query params `status` and `priority`.
2. `POST /tickets` to create a ticket. Validate required fields.
3. `GET /tickets/{id}` to fetch one ticket.
4. `PATCH /tickets/{id}` to update `subject`, `description`, `priority`, or `status`.
5. `DELETE /tickets/{id}` to delete a ticket.
6. `GET /metrics` returning counts by status and priority plus average age of open tickets in seconds.

Engineering requirements:
- Read/write a `tickets.json` file safely enough for sequential requests.
- Return JSON for all responses, including errors.
- Include CORS headers for local browser testing.
- Handle invalid JSON, unknown routes, unsupported methods, and missing IDs.
- Start on `127.0.0.1:8080` by default.

Output only the complete JavaScript code. Do not include explanations.

Create a single-file frontend app named `index.html`. Do not use external libraries, CDNs, images, or build tools. Keep the total code under 1,000 lines.

Task: build an accessible “meeting notes board” for a small team.

Functional requirements:
1. Users can add a note with title, owner, meeting date, tags, and body.
2. Notes appear as cards and can be filtered by owner, tag, and free-text search.
3. A note can be marked as “Action Required,” “Decision,” or “Reference.”
4. Users can edit and delete notes.
5. Persist all data in `localStorage`.
6. Provide a compact statistics strip: total notes, action-required count, unique owners, and most common tag.
7. Responsive layout: two-column card grid on wide screens, single column on narrow screens.
8. Basic accessibility: labels for inputs, keyboard-operable controls, visible focus states.

Output only the complete `index.html` content, including HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Do not include explanatory text.

Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary for modern business readers.

Output requirements:
1. A 100-word executive summary.
2. Five principles of effective business writing implied by the author.
3. A table with two columns: “Original-era advice” and “Modern equivalent.” Include at least five rows.
4. Identify any advice that may be dated and explain how to update it without losing the underlying principle.
5. Do not add claims that are not grounded in the passage.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” longer excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is the structured English summary for modern business readers, based solely on the provided passage.

### Executive Summary
Half of all business letters waste resources by being unnecessary or incoherent, often requiring six to eight exchanges instead of two. Effective correspondence demands clear thinking and planning; its primary goals are to convey an idea and build goodwill, accomplishing both courteously and concisely. Letters should not draw attention to themselves but to their message. Standard, tasteful formatting (plain white paper, simple letterheads) is preferred over gaudy or “original” designs. Each letter should cover one subject to aid filing, and addresses must be precise and complete. Carelessness—unsigned letters, missing enclosures, insufficient postage—damages credibility and wastes time.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing
1.  **Think before you write** – Clear, planned writing results from clear thinking, not speed.
2.  **Prioritize clarity and goodwill** – A letter must “get its idea across” and “build up good will.”
3.  **Be concise and complete** – The best letter achieves its goal in the “briefest space of time (and paper).”
4.  **Let the message, not the medium, shine** – Writing that calls attention to itself (style, looks) fails if it obscures the content.
5.  **Respect the reader’s time** – Every letter should have a reason (even a simple “thank you”) and avoid aimless effort.

### Original-era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-era Advice | Modern Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Use plain white paper of good quality; avoid gaudy colors and flashy designs. | Use clean, professional email templates and document formats; avoid excessive fonts, colors, or distracting graphics. |
| Use standard-size envelopes and opaque paper to conceal contents. | Use standard PDF attachments, clear subject lines, and professional email signatures; ensure no confidential data is visible in the preview pane. |
| A business letter should handle only one subject (file separately). | Send one email per distinct subject to enable clear threading, easy searching, and correct folder filing. |
| Include full state names (e.g., “New York” not “City”) and complete, correct postage. | Ensure complete “To,” “CC,” and “BCC” fields; use accurate email addresses and confirm attachments are included before sending. |
| Begin “Dear Mr. Brown” rather than “Dear Sir”; use honorary titles (Dr., Rev., Hon.). | Use the recipient’s preferred name and correct pronouns; avoid generic salutations like “To Whom It May Concern” when personalization is possible. |

### Dated Advice & How to Update It

**Dated advice:**  
“Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a ‘Help Wanted’ advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant’s own handwriting.”

**Why it is dated:**  
Business correspondence is now overwhelmingly digital (email, messaging apps), and handwritten responses are rare outside of specific creative or personal contexts.

**Updated modern principle:**  
*Choose the appropriate medium for professionalism and clarity.*  
- For most routine business communication, use typed digital formats (email, formal documents).  
- Reserve handwritten notes for high-touch, personal gestures (e.g., thank-you notes to key clients, condolence letters), where the personal effort builds goodwill—the same principle the original author valued. This preserves the underlying principle of selecting the format that best serves the message and the relationship.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)

Mary Lennox, a sickly, sour-faced nine-year-old, is sent to live with her uncle at Misselthwaite Manor after her parents die in a cholera outbreak in India. Born unwanted by her beauty-obsessed mother, Mary was raised by servants who obeyed her every whim, making her tyrannical and selfish. One hot morning, she awakens furious to find her Ayah replaced by a frightened stranger. As chaos erupts, Mary learns cholera has struck. Her mother panics with a young officer, then wailing announces the Ayah’s death. In the confusion, Mary hides alone in the nursery, forgotten. She eats leftover food, drinks wine, and falls into a deep, drowsy sleep, isolated and uncared for as death rages outside.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Concrete Details

- **Disagreeable and sour:** She has “a sour expression” and is called “the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen.”
- **Tyrannical and selfish:** By age six, she is “as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived” because servants always obeyed her.
- **Prone to violent tantrums:** When the strange servant says her Ayah cannot come, Mary “threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her.”
- **Racially insulting:** She mutters “Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” about a native, knowing it is “the worst insult of all.”
- **Neglected and forgotten:** During the cholera chaos, “Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her,” and she hides alone in the nursery.

### Setting’s Effect on Emotional Distance or Isolation

The setting—first a hot, mysterious morning in India during a cholera outbreak, then a deserted nursery—creates a profound sense of isolation. Mary is physically surrounded by servants yet emotionally abandoned: her mother “did not want a little girl at all” and kept her out of sight. During the crisis, the adults panic and flee, forgetting Mary entirely. The empty dining-room with hastily abandoned food and chairs emphasizes sudden, cold desertion. The nursery becomes a lonely prison where Mary cries, sleeps, and drinks wine alone, hearing “mysterious and frightening sounds” outside. No one seeks her or comforts her, deepening her emotional distance from every living person.

### Three Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)

1. Why do you think Mary’s mother handed her over to the Ayah and kept her “out of sight”? How might that have affected Mary’s behavior?
2. How did the servants’ constant obedience shape Mary into a “tyrannical and selfish” child? Can you think of a different way she might have grown up?
3. During the cholera outbreak, Mary is “forgotten by everyone.” How does being alone and unwanted make her feel, and what would you do if you were in her place?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes water’s constant movement across Earth, stored in oceans, atmosphere, land surface, and underground. Oceans hold 96% of all water; the rest is fresh water in lakes, rivers, ice, and groundwater. Water changes between liquid, solid, and gas as it moves via evaporation, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. The sun’s energy and gravity drive these processes. Human water use, land use, and climate change affect water movement, storage, and availability. Understanding these impacts helps promote sustainable water use.

### Concept Map (Text Form)
Sun’s energy → Evaporation/Evapotranspiration → Atmosphere (water vapor) → Precipitation → Land surface (snowpack, lakes, rivers) → Runoff/Streamflow → Oceans  
Atmosphere → Precipitation → Land surface → Infiltration → Groundwater (aquifers) → Discharge (springs, rivers, ocean)  
Oceans → Evaporation → Atmosphere → (cycle repeats)

### Key Terms and Definitions
1. **Water cycle**: The continuous movement and storage of water on Earth, changing between liquid, solid, and gas.  
2. **Evaporation**: The process where liquid water is transformed into water vapor by the sun’s energy and enters the atmosphere.  
3. **Evapotranspiration**: The main way water moves from land surfaces and oceans into the atmosphere, combining evaporation and plant transpiration.  
4. **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail that falls from clouds to Earth’s surface, driven by gravity.  
5. **Infiltration**: The movement of water from the land surface into the ground.  
6. **Groundwater recharge**: The process by which infiltrated water enters and refills underground aquifers.  
7. **Aquifer**: An underground layer where groundwater flows and is stored.  
8. **Runoff**: Water that flows across the land surface, eventually reaching streams, rivers, or oceans.

### Human Influence
Human actions affect the water cycle through water use (e.g., withdrawals for drinking, irrigation, or industry), land use changes (e.g., paving surfaces that reduce infiltration), and climate change (which alters precipitation patterns and evaporation rates). These impacts modify how much water is stored in various pools, the timing and speed of water movement, and water quality. Understanding these effects is essential for using water sustainably.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Executive Update: Shipping Delays & Customer Impact – What Happened, What’s Next  

**Team,**  

I want to give you a direct, honest update on the operational disruption we’ve experienced this week related to our warehouse management system (WMS) migration. By now, many of you have felt the downstream effects: slower shipping speeds, higher customer contacts, and a tougher-than-normal day for our frontline teams.  

As COO, I own the outcome of this migration. I want to explain what happened, where we are today, and what will change next week—without finger-pointing or fluff.  

### What Happened  

On Monday, we began the final cutover to our new WMS. This system has been in development for 18 months, tested across three pilot facilities, and signed off by our integration partner. Despite that preparation, the reality of a full-scale go-live introduced two specific issues:  

1. **Picking-path optimization failed in three of our largest distribution centers.** The new system’s routing logic, designed to reduce travel time, actually created bottlenecks in high-density SKU zones. Picking rates dropped by roughly 40% in those facilities during the first 48 hours.  
2. **Order-to-carrier handshake timing broke.** A minor API configuration error caused about 6% of packed orders to “ping” the carrier system incorrectly, delaying label generation and batch releases. This was resolved by Wednesday evening, but the cumulative lag pushed outbound volume into Thursday and Friday.  

These were not caused by any single person, team, or vendor. The new system’s logic was validated under simulation, but real-world SKU density and real-time carrier behavior were not fully replicable in testing. We identified the root cause late Tuesday, implemented a fix Thursday, and are back to pre-migration throughput as of this morning.  

### Current Customer Impact  

As of this writing (Friday, 4:00 PM):  

- **Shipping speed:** Orders placed Monday–Wednesday are averaging 1.8 days longer than our standard. Thursday and Friday orders are trending closer to normal, with a 0.4-day delay.  
- **Customer support volume:** Up roughly 3.2x vs. last week. The top three contact reasons are: “Where is my order?”, “I received a partial shipment,” and “Can I change my shipping method?”  
- **Partial shipments:** Approximately 2.4% of orders shipped with one or more items missing. This is typical during WMS transitions when pick paths are recalculated mid-wave. We are retroactively sending those items on priority (no extra charge to the customer).  

**Our customer-facing teams—support, care, and returns—are absorbing the weight of this.** They are dealing with frustrated customers while navigating tools that are still stabilizing. I want to be blunt: we are asking them to be patient and empathetic on behalf of a system issue that was not their fault. If you interact with any frontline team member this week, please thank them first.  

### What Is Being Done Today  

**Operations:**  
- All three affected DCs are running at target pick rates. The picking-path fix (manual rebalancing of high-density zones) is in place and stable.  
- Our carrier integration fix is confirmed. Every order placed after 10:00 AM Thursday has been transmitted correctly.  
- A “Priority Recovery” shift is working overtime tonight and tomorrow to clear the remaining 1,200 orders from Monday’s backlog.  

**Customer Support:**  
- We have brought in 12 additional cross-trained agents from our returns and chat teams to handle surge volume.  
- Live wait time is averaging 14 minutes (up from 4 minutes last week). We expect that to drop to <8 minutes by Monday evening as backlog orders start arriving and customers see tracking updates.  
- A temporary script has been added for agents: customers impacted by the partial-shipment issue will automatically receive a $10 credit without needing to ask. You can approve this retroactively.  

### What Will Change Next Week  

- **Monday:** We will implement a freeze on any new WMS feature releases for two weeks. No changes to picking logic, carrier integrations, or warehouse workflows until we confirm stability for seven consecutive days.  
- **Tuesday:** The Vendor Management team will present a post-mortem of the carrier-API failure to our infrastructure leads. That fix has already been documented, but we will add a redundant validation step for all future carrier handshakes.  
- **Wednesday–Friday:** A daily 10-minute check-in for all warehouse operations leads, customer support managers, and me. No slides—just “What’s broken, what’s fixed, what help is needed.”  
- **By next Friday:** We will send a company-wide update with the final customer impact metrics (returns, credits issued, NPS shift) and what we are doing to prevent a repeat.  

### How to Escalate Urgent Issues  

If you are on the frontline (warehouse, support, logistics, retail) and encounter an issue that is blocking your ability to serve a customer or do your job:  

- **During shift hours:** Contact your manager or shift lead. If they are unavailable or the issue is beyond their scope, ping **#ops-escalation** in Slack. A person will respond within 15 minutes.  
- **After hours (7 PM–7 AM ET):** Call the on-call operations lead at **+1-555-202-4800**. That number rings directly to a rotating manager who has authority to pause a process or approve a temporary workaround.  
- **For customer-specific refund/credit issues that exceed standard policy:** Use the **#cs-escalation** channel. Approvals for amounts up to $50 are pre-authorized this week.  

No one should feel like they have to “wait for approval” while a customer is on the line. Use your judgment. We will back you.  

### Manager Talking Points (4 Bullets)  

If a team member asks you what to tell customers or colleagues, here is the concise version:  

1. **We had two specific technical failures in our WMS migration—picking path logic and carrier API timing. Both are fixed. Throughput is back to normal as of today.**  
2. **Customers who ordered Monday–Wednesday will experience a 1- to 2-day delay. Thursday and Friday orders are mostly on time. Partial shipments are being re-sent immediately, with a $10 credit auto-applied.**  
3. **Support volume is high but manageable. Wait times are around 14 minutes. We have added extra agents and a pre-approved credit policy to reduce friction. The backlog clears this weekend.**  
4. **There is a 15-minute escalation window during shifts (via #ops-escalation) and a 24/7 on-call number for after-hours. Approvals up to $50 are pre-authorized this week. You do not need permission to help a customer who is truly stuck.**

### Closing  

This week was a gut-check. We set a high bar for reliability, and we missed it. I take responsibility for the decision to proceed with the cutover on the timeline we chose. Our teams—especially in operations and customer support—performed with professionalism under pressure. That is not a platitude; it is observable fact.  

Next week, we will be more conservative with change, more transparent about risk, and more responsive when something breaks. That is the standard we will hold ourselves to.  

Thank you for your patience and for showing up this week.  

Sincerely,  
[Your Name]  
COO, [Company Name]

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
Here is a story for children ages 7–10.

---

### The Map of Puddles and the Sparrow Who Forgot to Land

The rain had stopped. The world smelled of wet earth and clean leaves. In a quiet corner of the garden, behind the rhubarb patch, a small snail named Noa was very, very busy.

Noa was a careful snail. You could even call him a *professional* careful snail. While other snails slid around without a plan, Noa had a system. He carried his home on his back, of course, but he also carried something else: a rolled-up leaf he called his Map of Puddles.

Today, after the summer rain, the garden was a map-maker’s dream. Noa unrolled his leaf and looked at the tiny scratches and lines he had made. “Ah,” he said to himself, his voice a soft whisper. “Puddle Number Nine is here, by the forget-me-nots. It’s shaped like a bean. And Puddle Number Four is under the big rose bush. It’s deep and clear.”

He crawled slowly toward Puddle Four, his slimy trail shining like a silver thread. Every few minutes, he stopped and made a tiny mark on his map. He noted the water’s depth, its coolness, and the way a single fallen petal floated on top. For Noa, knowing exactly where everything was—especially puddles—was the safest way to live. A dry patch of sun could be dangerous. A forgotten crack could be a trap. But puddles? Puddles were perfect drinking spots and places to cool his foot.

Just as he was measuring Puddle Four’s width with his antennae, a blur of brown feathers crashed into the wet grass beside him. *Whump!*

“OOF!” chirped a small voice.

Noa ducked into his shell. He waited. Slowly, he peeked out. A young sparrow was lying in a clump of damp clover, her wings splayed out like a wrinkled cape. She blinked at the sky. Her name was Pip.

Pip scrambled to her feet, shaking off water drops. She didn’t seem hurt, just annoyed. “Blast!” she chirped. “I almost had it that time! I was *ten* worm lengths above the ground!”

“Almost had what?” asked Noa, in his gentle, slide-y voice.

Pip looked down. She had never really talked to a snail before. “Flying!” she said. “I want to fly high, like the swallows. I want to touch the clouds! But every time I try, I… I just crash.”

“Where do you want to land?” asked Noa.

Pip tilted her head. “Land? I don’t land. I just… stop moving. And then I fall.”

Noa unrolled his map a little more. “That’s your problem,” he said wisely. “You can’t just fly. You have to learn where you’re going to stop.”

Pip hopped closer. “What’s that?” she asked, pointing at the leaf.

“This is my Map of Puddles,” said Noa proudly. “Every puddle in this garden. I know their shapes. I know their smells. I know if they have a friendly frog or a sharp twig. Knowing the map makes me brave.”

“Brave?” laughed Pip. “You move slower than the rain. I’m the one who’s brave! I try to fly right into the sky!”

Noa smiled his snail-smile. “Trying to fly is brave. But knowing how to land is also brave. It’s a different kind of brave. It’s the brave of being patient. My puddles are my landing spots. Without them, I’d just slide into danger.”

Pip thought about this. She had zoomed from a branch to a fence, from a fence to the ground, but she had never *planned* where she would come down. She just went, and then—*blam*—the ground surprised her.

“Show me your puddles,” she said.

Noa led her—very, very slowly—around the garden. He showed her Puddle Number One (shallow, for a quick drink), and Puddle Number Seven (deep, with a pebble in the middle that made a good seat). He explained how puddles lasted. “After a summer rain,” he said, “the sun comes out and drinks the water. A puddle shrinks from the edges. The middle is the last part to stay wet. If you watch the puddle, you can see it disappear, bit by bit. That’s how the world works. The sun and the air, they drink the water until it’s gone.”

Pip watched a puddle’s edge grow smaller in a patch of sunlight. “It’s like the puddle is breathing out,” she whispered.

“Exactly,” said Noa.

Then Noa had an idea. “You want to fly. But you need a soft place to stop. A puddle is soft. You could aim for the middle.”

Pip’s eyes grew wide. “A puddle landing?”

It sounded silly, but it was a plan. Noa pointed to Puddle Number Four, under the rose bush. “This is my favorite. It’s deep. The water will catch you like a green leaf catches a raindrop. If you come down here, you’ll get a splash, not a crash.”

Pip hopped onto a low rock. She fluffed her feathers. Her heart was beating fast, but not with the usual nervous flutter. This was a different feeling. It felt like waiting. It felt like patience.

She watched the puddle. She watched how the water was still. She picked her spot: the very center, where the petal floated.

“Ready?” asked Noa.

Pip took a deep breath. She flapped her wings. Up she went—ten worm lengths, then twenty! The garden looked like a green quilt. The wind blew beneath her, soft and steady. *I am flying,* she thought.

But then came the scary part. The staying-up part was fun. The coming-down part was hard. She remembered Noa’s words. *The brave of being patient.* She didn’t panic. She tilted her wings just a little. She aimed.

And she glided down, not like a falling rock, but like a floating leaf. She aimed for the center of Puddle Number Four.

*Splash!*

It was the most wonderful sound. The cool water kissed her feet. The petal bobbed beside her. She was not hurt. She was not scared. She was standing in a perfect, soft puddle.

“You did it!” cheered Noa, poking his head out from under a rose leaf.

Pip shook the water from her wingtips. “I did it! I didn’t just fly. I *arrived*!”

She hopped out of the puddle and sat beside Noa. “Thank you,” she said. “I thought bravery was just going fast. But you showed me that bravery is also going slow. It’s looking first. It’s knowing where you’ll put your feet—or your wings.”

Noa finished writing a tiny note on his map: *Puddle Four. Home of Pip’s first landing.*

“Now,” he said gently, “do you want to learn the map of the whole garden? We have seventeen more puddles to visit. And they’re not going anywhere fast.”

Pip laughed. “I’m not going anywhere fast either,” she said. And she tucked her wings in tight and began to walk, step by careful step, beside her very patient, very brave new friend.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Opens New ‘Pollinator Path’ to Inspire Community-Wide Habitat Connections**

**HARBORLEAF, NY – July 1, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden today announced the launch of its new “Pollinator Path,” a dedicated public education trail designed to teach visitors how to support bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds through thoughtful planting choices. The trail, located on the east meadow loop, will officially open to the public on July 12, 2026, offering a 0.8-mile accessible route featuring native plant demonstration beds, a night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, and youth observation cards.

Developed in direct response to visitor requests for practical, actionable guidance on creating pollinator-friendly habitats, the Pollinator Path aims to bridge the gap between botanical garden displays and everyday home gardening. The trail’s design emphasizes accessibility and hands-on learning, making it suitable for families, school groups, and individuals of all mobility levels.

“Our visitors have consistently asked for more than just beautiful displays—they want to know what they can actually do in their own yards, balconies, or community spaces to help pollinators thrive,” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “Small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods. One window box with native milkweed can support a monarch caterpillar, and a cluster of late-blooming asters can fuel a migrating hummingbird. The Pollinator Path shows how those individual actions link together.”

The trail begins at the east meadow loop entrance, winding through a series of curated demonstration beds. Each bed highlights a specific type of native plant—such as goldenrod, bee balm, and wild columbine—alongside interpretive signage that explains the plant’s relationship to particular pollinators. A dedicated night-blooming flower station, featuring moonflowers and evening primrose, will open after sunset on select evenings, allowing visitors to observe moths and nocturnal pollinators in action.

To ensure inclusivity, all signage on the Pollinator Path is tactile and includes Braille and large-print text, as well as raised illustrations of key plants and insects. Youth observation cards, available at the trailhead, encourage children to document the pollinators they spot, with guided prompts for sketching and note-taking. Shaded resting points, equipped with benches and water stations, are spaced every 200 feet along the route to accommodate visitors who may need breaks.

The path is designed to be self-guided, with interpretive markers at each station. On weekends, Harborleaf will offer volunteer-led walks, led by trained garden docents, that provide deeper insights into pollinator behavior, plant life cycles, and seasonal care tips. The walks are free with garden admission and will run every Saturday and Sunday morning from 10:00 a.m. to 11:30 a.m., beginning July 13.

The east meadow loop itself has been under restoration for two years, with a focus on replacing non-native grasses with a mix of wildflowers and shrubs that provide nectar, pollen, and larval host plants. The Pollinator Path occupies the northern half of the loop, leaving the southern section open for passive recreation and wildlife observation. According to Harborleaf staff, the entire trail is wheelchair- and stroller-friendly, with a compacted gravel surface and gentle slopes.

The impetus for the trail came from a survey conducted by Harborleaf in late 2025, in which nearly 60 percent of respondents indicated they wanted more information on how to create pollinator-friendly spaces at home. Many cited confusion over which plants to choose, when to plant, and how to avoid harming beneficial insects. The Pollinator Path aims to address these questions directly, with signage that explains topics such as pesticide-free gardening, the importance of bloom succession, and the role of dead wood and leaf litter as overwintering habitat.

“This is not about grand gestures,” Ortiz added. “It’s about recognizing that a suburban lawn, a school garden bed, or a community park can all be part of a larger network. Pollinators don’t see property lines. When we plant for them, we build corridors of survival.”

Harborleaf Botanical Garden is also distributing free take-home guides at the trailhead, which list recommended native plants for the region, planting timelines, and maintenance tips. The guides are available in English and Spanish.

The Pollinator Path opens on July 12, 2026, at 9:00 a.m. with a brief ribbon-cutting ceremony at the east meadow loop entrance. Garden staff will be on hand to answer questions, and the first 50 visitors will receive a complimentary packet of native wildflower seeds. The trail will remain open daily from dawn to dusk, year-round, with no additional fee beyond regular garden admission.

Harborleaf Botanical Garden is located at 742 Meadow Lane, Harborleaf, NY. For trail maps, accessibility information, and the full schedule of volunteer-led walks, visitors are encouraged to visit the garden’s website or contact the education department directly.

**Media Contact:**
Elena Vasquez  
Communications Manager  
Harborleaf Botanical Garden  
Phone: (555) 418-9205  
Email: evasquez@harborleafgarden.org  
Website: www.harborleafgarden.org/pollinator-path

###

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
