Write a single-file Node.js HTTP server named `server.js`. Use only built-in Node.js modules; no Express, no npm packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Task: implement a small JSON API for a help-desk ticket queue stored in a local JSON file.

Data model:
- Ticket: `id`, `subject`, `description`, `priority` (`low`, `medium`, `high`), `status` (`open`, `in_progress`, `resolved`), `created_at`, `updated_at`.

API requirements:
1. `GET /tickets` with optional query params `status` and `priority`.
2. `POST /tickets` to create a ticket. Validate required fields.
3. `GET /tickets/{id}` to fetch one ticket.
4. `PATCH /tickets/{id}` to update `subject`, `description`, `priority`, or `status`.
5. `DELETE /tickets/{id}` to delete a ticket.
6. `GET /metrics` returning counts by status and priority plus average age of open tickets in seconds.

Engineering requirements:
- Read/write a `tickets.json` file safely enough for sequential requests.
- Return JSON for all responses, including errors.
- Include CORS headers for local browser testing.
- Handle invalid JSON, unknown routes, unsupported methods, and missing IDs.
- Start on `127.0.0.1:8080` by default.

Output only the complete JavaScript code. Do not include explanations.

Create a single-file frontend app named `index.html`. Do not use external libraries, CDNs, images, or build tools. Keep the total code under 1,000 lines.

Task: build an accessible “meeting notes board” for a small team.

Functional requirements:
1. Users can add a note with title, owner, meeting date, tags, and body.
2. Notes appear as cards and can be filtered by owner, tag, and free-text search.
3. A note can be marked as “Action Required,” “Decision,” or “Reference.”
4. Users can edit and delete notes.
5. Persist all data in `localStorage`.
6. Provide a compact statistics strip: total notes, action-required count, unique owners, and most common tag.
7. Responsive layout: two-column card grid on wide screens, single column on narrow screens.
8. Basic accessibility: labels for inputs, keyboard-operable controls, visible focus states.

Output only the complete `index.html` content, including HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Do not include explanatory text.

Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary for modern business readers.

Output requirements:
1. A 100-word executive summary.
2. Five principles of effective business writing implied by the author.
3. A table with two columns: “Original-era advice” and “Modern equivalent.” Include at least five rows.
4. Identify any advice that may be dated and explain how to update it without losing the underlying principle.
5. Do not add claims that are not grounded in the passage.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” longer excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is the structured English summary for modern business readers, based solely on the provided passage.

### Executive Summary (100 words)

Half of all business letters are unnecessary, and most of the remainder are so poorly written that simple transactions require multiple clarifications. Effective writing stems from clear thinking and careful planning. A letter must serve two essential purposes: clearly convey its message and build goodwill for the sender. Many resources are wasted on aimless correspondence that yields no result. A letter fails if it draws attention to itself rather than its content; praise for style over substance indicates a miss. Standard formats are practical, while excessive originality appears unprofessional. A letter should cover only one subject to aid filing, and every detail—from address accuracy to proper postage—demands attention. Careless errors, such as unsigned letters or missing enclosures, undermine credibility.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing

1.  **Purpose Before Action:** Every letter should have a clear reason (e.g., thanking a customer) and is often unnecessary. Eliminate correspondence that does not serve a defined business goal.
2.  **Message over Medium:** The content is paramount. The letter must never attract attention to itself or its style; it exists only to deliver its message effectively.
3.  **Get the Idea Across:** Clarity and completeness are non-negotiable. A letter must succeed in transmitting its core idea to avoid extended, multi-letter transactions.
4.  **Build Goodwill While Being Concise:** The best letters achieve their objective courteously and completely in the briefest time and space, balancing efficiency with relationship-building.
5.  **Respect the Reader’s Time and Attention:** Proper formatting, single-subject focus, accurate addressing, and complete enclosures all demonstrate consideration for the recipient and prevent wasted effort.

### Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-era advice | Modern equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| “Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Gaudy colors, flashy designs… are taboo.” | Use clean, professional templates. Avoid distracting or overly decorative fonts, colors, or backgrounds in digital documents or emails. |
| “Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery… The first thing most people want to know… is what sort of looking place it is.” | Use branded visuals strategically. A company logo, product image, or office photo in a signature or slide deck is effective when it answers a specific reader question. |
| “A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered.” | Use a clear subject line per message. For multiple topics, send separate emails or use distinct subject lines to enable easy filing and search. |
| “The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around.” | Ensure digital documents are well-formatted with consistent spacing, proper line breaks, and sufficient whitespace for readability on screen. |
| “Special care should be taken with the outside address… State abbreviations should be used sparingly… ‘City’ is not sufficient.” | Double-check all recipient details: email addresses, full names, job titles, and company names. Avoid ambiguous or incomplete contact fields. |

### Dated Advice and How to Update It

**Dated advice:** “The typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws…”

**Underlying principle:** The person who sends the communication is ultimately responsible for its quality and should not delegate full oversight to an untrained assistant. The originator must know what good work looks like to give effective feedback.

**How to update it without losing the principle:** Rephrase the statement to be gender-neutral and collaborative. Instead of implying that one role (e.g., “typist”) is inherently irresponsible, frame it as a shared responsibility. The modern equivalent: “The author of any business communication is responsible for its final quality. They should clearly review drafts from assistants, proofread before sending, and ensure they can articulate what makes a message clear and professional. Regular feedback loops with support staff build competence, not blame.”

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
Mary Lennox, a sickly, sour-faced nine-year-old, is sent to live with her uncle after her parents die in a cholera outbreak in India. Born unwanted by her beauty-obsessed mother, Mary was raised by servants who obeyed her every whim, making her tyrannical and selfish. One hot morning, she awakens cross to find a strange servant instead of her Ayah. She kicks and beats the woman in rage. Later, she overhears her mother speaking fearfully with a young officer about a deadly cholera outbreak. Servants begin wailing as the Ayah dies. Panic spreads; people die rapidly. Mary hides in the nursery, forgotten by everyone. She eats leftover food, drinks wine, and falls into a deep sleep, alone and uncared for.

### Mary’s Character Traits (with Concrete Details)
- **Disagreeable-looking**: She has a thin face, thin body, thin light hair, and a sour expression.
- **Tyrannical**: She beats and kicks the strange servant who replaces her Ayah.
- **Selfish**: She calls the native servants “Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” when angry.
- **Fretful and cross**: She awakens feeling “very cross” on the hot morning.
- **Ignored and unwanted**: No one thinks of her during the cholera crisis; she hides alone in the nursery.

### Setting and Emotional Distance
The story opens in colonial India during a cholera epidemic, where Mary is physically isolated in a bungalow nursery while chaos rages outside. The heat, mysterious absences of servants, and wailing from huts create a frightening, bewildering atmosphere. Mary is forgotten by everyone, including her mother, who never wanted her. This neglect deepens Mary’s emotional isolation, as she must fend for herself without comfort or care, eating leftovers and drinking wine alone.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary behaves so badly—kicking and insulting the servants? Is it her fault?
2. How would you feel if you were Mary, left alone while scary things happened around you?
3. Do you think Mary’s mother loved her? Use details from the story to explain your answer.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise 100-Word Overview
Water is in constant motion across Earth, stored in oceans, atmosphere, land surface, and underground. It changes between liquid, solid, and gas forms. Oceans hold 96% of all water (salty); the rest is fresh, stored in lakes, rivers, ice, snow, and groundwater. Movement is driven by solar energy and gravity: evaporation and evapotranspiration send water to the atmosphere; precipitation returns it; runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow transport it across and below the surface. Humans affect the water cycle through water use and land use, impacting storage, movement, and availability. Understanding these impacts supports sustainable water use.

### Concept Map (Text Form)
Atmosphere ↔ Evaporation / Evapotranspiration → Atmosphere  
↓ Precipitation  
Land Surface (Lakes, Rivers, Snow, Ice) → Runoff → Streamflow → Oceans  
↓ Infiltration  
Groundwater (Aquifers) → Groundwater Flow → Natural Discharge (Rivers, Oceans, Springs)

### Key Terms (Based on Passage)
1. **Water cycle**: The continuous movement and storage of water on Earth.  
2. **Evaporation**: The process by which liquid water turns into water vapor due to solar energy.  
3. **Evapotranspiration**: The main way water moves from land and oceans into the atmosphere.  
4. **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail falling from clouds due to gravity.  
5. **Infiltration**: The movement of water from the surface into the ground.  
6. **Groundwater recharge**: The process by which water enters underground aquifers.  
7. **Aquifer**: Underground rock formations that store and transmit groundwater.  
8. **Runoff**: Water that flows across the land surface toward streams or rivers.

### Human Influence
Human water use and land use affect how water moves, where it is stored, and how much is available. These actions alter natural water flow, change groundwater recharge rates, and modify the timing and amount of water in pools. By recognizing these impacts, humans can work toward using water sustainably.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Operations Update: Warehouse System Migration and Current Service Impact  

**Team,**  

I’m writing to give you a direct, honest update on our recent warehouse management system migration and the service challenges it has created this week. This is not a message of blame—it’s one of accountability, transparency, and a clear plan forward.  

**What happened**  
Last weekend, we completed the planned transition from our legacy warehouse system to a new platform designed to support our growth and improve long-term order accuracy. The migration required re-mapping thousands of product locations, updating pick-and-pack workflows, and retraining our fulfillment teams on new handheld scanners and software interfaces.  

Despite extensive testing and a phased rollout, the transition introduced unexpected latency in real-time inventory updates. When the new system went live, our fulfillment center staff encountered slower scan-to-ship times, occasional mismatches between system inventory and physical stock, and a learning curve for new screen navigation. This reduced our daily shipping capacity by roughly 30% for the first five days, with gradual improvement each day.  

**Current customer impact**  
As of this morning, we are approximately 1,200 orders behind our normal shipping cadence. Standard delivery estimates shown on our site are currently out of date by 2–3 business days. Customer support volume has increased by 60% this week, primarily from order-status inquiries and delay-related frustration. Our support team is handling roughly 400 additional tickets per day.  

To be clear: every single order will ship. No orders have been lost, canceled, or corrupted by the migration. The delay is purely operational throughput.  

**What is being done today**  
1. **Warehouse floor:** We have deployed three senior system integrators from the software vendor on-site. They are running script patches to reduce scan latency and manually reconciling the top 50 inventory location errors identified this morning.  
2. **Shipping prioritization:** We are using a manual override to process any order placed five or more days ago first, regardless of product type.  
3. **Customer support:** A cross-functional team from our CX and operations leads is meeting hourly to update the support knowledge base with real-time delay windows. We’ve also enabled a temporary “delay notification” banner on order-confirmation pages and within tracking emails.  
4. **Overtime authorized:** All overtime requests in the warehouse and support teams are pre-approved through next Friday, with no cap.  

**What will change next week**  
By Monday morning, we expect scan latency to be reduced to under two seconds per item (from the current six-to-ten-second average). The vendor is deploying a targeted patch this Saturday during our overnight window.  

On Tuesday, we will begin a catch-up sprint using a modified pick path that routes high-volume, single-item orders to a dedicated team using paper barcode sheets as a backup until scanners stabilize. This should clear the backlog of 1,200 orders by Wednesday end of day.  

On Thursday, we will restore normal shipping estimates on our site and begin returning to standard order-to-ship windows.  

**How to escalate urgent issues**  
If you are on the warehouse floor and encounter a system lockout, inventory count discrepancy that stops a pick, or a safety issue, escalate immediately to your shift manager, who will page **Alex Chen (Head of Fulfillment)** and **the on-call vendor engineer** via the #ops-escalation Slack channel. Please title your message with “[SHIP ESCALATION]” so it is flagged.  

If you are in customer support and receive a report of a high-risk order (e.g., fragile goods at a critical delivery deadline), flag it in Zendesk with the tag **“COO_URGENT”** and send the ticket ID to me directly. I will review and route within 30 minutes.  

**Manager talking points**  
For anyone leading a team in this environment—here are four concise points you can share in stand-ups or one-on-ones:  

- **The migration was necessary, but the transition has been slower than expected.** We are not finger-pointing; we are fixing.  
- **Overtime is fully approved through next Friday.** No manager should deny a request if it keeps orders moving or customers supported.  
- **Every customer will receive their order.** We know they are frustrated, but we also know they will see our commitment to getting it right.  
- **Escalation works.** If something blocks your team from doing their job, escalate with the tags and channels above—not later, now.  

**A note of respect**  
To our warehouse associates who are navigating a new system while pulling double the normal pick rate: thank you. To our customer support team absorbing 400 extra tickets with patience and empathy: thank you. To the engineering and operations leads who have been on calls since 5 a.m. every day: thank you.  

I’ll send a brief update Friday afternoon with ship-rate data and projected recovery timing. Until then, please keep communicating openly with each other—and with me if something doesn’t feel right.  

We built this company on solving problems for customers. Right now, we solve the biggest problem first: getting orders out the door.  

—  
**[Your Name]**  
Chief Operating Officer

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
Here is a story for children ages 7–10, written to your specifications.

---

### The Snail Who Mapped the Puddles and the Sparrow Who Forgot to Land

The summer rain had stopped, leaving the whole world smelling like wet stone and cut grass. In the garden behind the little blue house, the world was a million tiny mirrors. Puddles glittered on the broad leaves of the rhubarb, perched in the cups of the tulips, and filled the dips in the cracked stone path.

At the edge of the path lived a snail named Silas. Silas was not a fast snail, and he was proud of it. He was a careful snail. In his head, and on a smooth, flat pebble he carried on his shell, he kept the most important map in the world: **The Great Puddle Atlas of the Garden.**

“A puddle is a beautiful thing,” Silas would say to no one in particular as he traced a silvery path across the stone. “But it can be a danger. A fern frond can hide a deep one. A sunny spot will have a shallow one that dries by noon. A wise traveler must know the difference.”

He had just finished marking a new, particularly deep puddle under the dripping eaves of the garden shed. He drew a careful swirl on his pebble. *“The Deep Blue of the Shed’s Shadow,”* he called it.

High above him, on the clothesline, sat Pip. Pip was a young sparrow, brown and fuzzy, with bright, eager eyes. She had been watching the clouds scoot by for days, watching the older birds ride the wind. They would leap from the line, spread their wings, and whoosh! They became part of the sky.

Pip wanted that. She wanted it *now*.

Her mother had told her a hundred times, “First, you must flap. Then you must learn to flutter. *Then* you learn to glide. And last of all, you must learn to land. Landing is the most important part.”

But landing seemed so boring. Flying was the brave part.

Today, with the sun warming the wet garden and a perfect, gentle breeze blowing from the rose bushes, Pip felt the pull of the sky stronger than ever. “I’m ready,” she chirped to the empty air. “I’ll just fly a little bit. A tiny loop. I can figure out landing when I get back.”

She took a deep breath, wobbled on the line, and then—with a hopeful squawk—she jumped.

For a glorious two seconds, Pip flew. The wind caught her under her stubby wings and lifted her over the petunias. She was free! She was a sky-sparrow!

Then, the wind dropped her.

She flapped frantically, her heart a tiny drum. She wasn’t steering. She wasn’t gliding. She was just falling. Down, down, down, past the lavender, past the birdbath, and with a *splat* and a *fizzle*, she crashed directly into the middle of The Deep Blue of the Shed’s Shadow.

Water went up her beak. Mud splashed in her eyes. She was not flying. She was sitting in a cold, wet puddle, covered in leaf sludge.

“Oh, dear,” said a very slow, very calm voice.

Pip blinked the mud away and saw a snail with a pebble on his shell staring at her from the puddle’s edge.

“You have landed in ‘The Deep Blue of the Shed’s Shadow’,” Silas said, consulting his map. “Maximum depth: one and a half acorns. Risk: high. Current status: occupied by a damp sparrow.”

Pip tried to hop out, but her feet were stuck in the muddy bottom. She shivered. “I was flying,” she whispered, ashamed.

“And you forgot to learn how to stop,” Silas said, nodding wisely. “A common mistake of the young and hasty.”

“I just wanted to be brave,” Pip said, a fat tear rolling down her cheek and plopping into the puddle.

Silas looked at the puddle, then at the sky. “Patience can be a kind of bravery too,” he said. “The bravest thing is not always the fast thing. The bravest thing can be waiting until you are ready.”

Pip sniffled. “But how will I get out?”

“You will wait,” said Silas. “And I will show you a wonder.”

They waited. The sun, which had been hiding, came out from behind a cloud and shone directly on The Deep Blue of the Shed’s Shadow.

“Watch the edges,” Silas said.

Pip watched. The water at the very edge of the puddle, where it met the hot, wet concrete, began to shiver. It shimmered and grew thin. A tiny patch of concrete appeared. Then another. The puddle was shrinking!

“The sun is a thirsty giant,” Silas said. “It drinks the puddles with its warm breath. It takes the water up into the sky, where it becomes a cloud, which will rain again another day.”

The water around Pip’s feet was getting shallower. The mud felt firmer. With one last, careful step, Pip’s claws found the bottom. She hopped out onto the dry path, dripping and free.

She looked back at the puddle, which was now just a dark, wet circle. “It went away,” she breathed.

“It became something else,” said Silas. “It had the patience to become a cloud. Everything worth doing takes its time.”

Pip looked at the clothesline. It seemed so far away. She flapped her wings once, gently. She did not jump. Instead, she hopped onto a low brick, then to the handle of a watering can, then to a fencepost. She took her time.

“That was a very good climb,” said Silas from his new spot on a sunny stone.

“It wasn’t flying,” said Pip.

“No,” agreed Silas. “But it was brave. Because you were patient. You waited until you were steady.”

Pip looked at the snail’s map. She could see all the careful swirls and markings. It had taken the snail a whole summer to make that map. He hadn’t rushed.

From that day on, Pip practiced. She flapped on the clothesline for hours. She fluttered from the fencepost to the birdbath. She learned to glide on the breeze, feeling how it lifted her. And last of all, with her mother’s help, she learned the hardest lesson of all: how to spread her tail and put her feet down just at the right moment to land softly on a branch.

And when she finally did fly, really fly, all the way around the garden and back, she swooped down and landed right beside Silas on his sunny stone.

“Good landing,” said the snail.

“Thank you,” said the sparrow. “I waited until I was ready.”

And Silas, the careful snail, took out his pebble and drew a new symbol on the very bottom corner. It wasn’t a puddle. It was a tiny, perfect pair of wings. He labeled it *“The Brave Landing of Pip.”*

Because he knew that sometimes, the most important thing to map is not a place, but how you get there.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Unveils New ‘Pollinator Path’ Trail Focused on Habitat Education**

**HARBORLEAF, MA – June 10, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden today announced the opening of the “Pollinator Path,” a new 0.8-mile public education trail designed to teach visitors how to support bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds through native planting. The trail, situated along the east meadow loop, will officially open on July 12, 2026, and is fully accessible to visitors of all mobility levels.

The Pollinator Path emerges in direct response to a growing trend among Harborleaf visitors: a desire for practical, actionable guidance on how to create pollinator-friendly habitats at home. Garden staff have noted a sharp increase in questions about which plants best support local pollinators, how to design gardens that provide year-round resources, and what small-scale changes can make a measurable difference for declining insect and bird populations.

“Our visitors come to us inspired by the beauty of our collections, but they often leave wanting to do more,” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “They ask, ‘What can I actually plant in my backyard? What will help the bees and butterflies I see here?’ The Pollinator Path is our answer. It shows that small planting decisions, when multiplied across a neighborhood, can create connected corridors of habitat that sustain entire pollinator communities.”

The trail has been designed as an immersive, self-guided educational experience. Its features include:

- **Native Plant Demonstration Beds:** Eleven distinct planting zones showcase native perennials, shrubs, and grasses that provide nectar, pollen, and larval host plants for key pollinator species. Each bed is labeled with common and scientific names, bloom periods, and the specific pollinators it attracts.
- **Night-Blooming Flower Station:** A dedicated area highlights plants such as evening primrose, moonflower, and night-blooming jasmine, illustrating the critical role of moths and nocturnal insects in nighttime pollination.
- **Tactile Signage:** All interpretive panels incorporate raised text, botanical illustrations, and textured elements (such as embossed leaf shapes and seed pod replicas) to ensure the trail is fully accessible to visitors with visual impairments.
- **Youth Observation Cards:** Children and families can pick up free, laminated observation cards at the trailhead. The cards prompt young visitors to record pollinator sightings, identify flower shapes, and note the time of day different insects appear.
- **Shaded Resting Points:** Four bench-lined rest areas, shaded by native oak and serviceberry trees, offer quiet spots for reflection and observation. Each rest point includes a small interpretive panel with a “Did You Know?” pollinator fact.
- **Volunteer-Led Weekend Walks:** Starting July 12, trained garden docents will lead free, 45-minute guided walks along the Pollinator Path every Saturday and Sunday at 10:00 a.m. and 2:00 p.m. through October. Walks cover plant identification, pollinator life cycles, and simple at-home gardening techniques.

The trail’s design emphasizes low-impact, sustainable landscaping. All plants were sourced from regional native nurseries, and the path surface is made of compacted, permeable gravel to reduce runoff. The Garden’s horticulture team worked closely with the extension service of a nearby university to ensure the demonstration beds reflect ecologically sound planting combinations for the local climate zone.

Ortiz noted that the Pollinator Path is not intended as a lecture, but as a starting point for individual action. “You don’t need a large garden or a costly redesign to make a difference,” she said. “A single pot of native milkweed on an apartment balcony can support monarch caterpillars. A patch of wild bergamot in a front yard can feed bumblebees for weeks. The Pollinator Path shows these choices, celebrates them, and gives people the confidence to try.”

The trail opens during a period of increased public awareness about pollinator decline. While Harborleaf Botanical Garden does not engage in advocacy or political campaigns, its educational mission is grounded in the science of ecology. The Garden’s leadership emphasized that the Pollinator Path is a nonpartisan, science-based resource available to all community members.

“Pollinators don’t care about political boundaries,” Ortiz added. “They move across fences, roads, and yards. When we make small, thoughtful planting decisions, we are connecting our landscapes to a larger, living network. That is what this trail is about: connection.”

Harborleaf Botanical Garden is a private, nonprofit institution dedicated to plant conservation, education, and horticultural display. The Garden’s 42-acre campus features themed collections, a greenhouse complex, and a year-round calendar of programs for adults, families, and school groups. The Pollinator Path was funded entirely through the Garden’s general operating budget and existing volunteer programs.

**Opening Day Details**
The Pollinator Path will be accessible during regular Garden hours (9:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m.) beginning July 12, 2026. Admission to the trail is included with general Garden entry. Youth observation cards and a printable trail map will be available at the welcome center. The weekend volunteer-led walks begin at 10:00 a.m. and 2:00 p.m. on opening day.

**Media Contact:**
Sarah Chen
Communications Manager
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
555-0142
s.chen@harborleafbg.org
www.harborleafbg.org/pollinatorpath

###

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The laundromat on Harbor Street smelled of bleach, salt, and the particular staleness of trapped coastal air. The power had been out for forty minutes. A battery-powered Coleman lantern sat on the folding counter, casting a cold, white bubble of light against the dark of the machines.

Lena had been here for the duration of her spin cycle when the lights flickered and died. Now her towels sat in a half-finished load, a dark mass glimpsed through the wet porthole of machine number seven.

She’d been staring at them when the door chimed, and a man walked in carrying a mesh bag of laundry and the automatic assumption that the power would be on.

He stopped two steps inside.

“Lena.”

She recognized the voice before she placed the face. It was rounder now. Kinder. The jawline had softened, and there were threads of gray in the brown hair that used to fall over his forehead. She’d known him at twenty-three, when they were neighbors on the third floor of a walk-up in the East End. He’d been a cook then. She’d been a graduate student who never graduated.

“Eli,” she said.

He looked at the lantern, then at the dark machines. “Huh.” He set his bag down on the scuffed linoleum. “This is. Unexpected.”

She turned back to the machine, as if the towels might become interesting. “The power went out about forty minutes ago. The attendant said the whole block is down. A truck hit a pole on Ocean Avenue.”

“Right.” He was still standing in the doorway. The carroty light of a distant emergency vehicle flickered through the front window, painting his silhouette for a moment before fading. “I’m just here because my hot water heater died. Figured I’d do a load while I wait for the plumber tomorrow.”

“Bad luck,” she said.

“Or good.” He moved closer, into the circle of the lantern, and sat on the bench against the wall. The vinyl creaked. “Depends on how you look at it.”

She didn’t answer. She didn’t know how to look at it. She hadn’t seen him in seven years, and the last time she’d seen him, she’d been standing in the hallway of their building with a suitcase, and she’d watched him through the crack of his slightly open door and decided not to knock.

“How’ve you been?” he asked.

It was the most ordinary question in the world, and it sat between them like an object of unmanageable weight.

“Fine,” she said. “Working at the library. The one near the pier.”

“I know it. I walk past it sometimes.” He paused. “I mean. Not on purpose. I mean, I walk past it. To the pier.”

“That’s good,” she said. “Walking’s good.”

They sat in silence. The lantern hummed faintly. A moth bumped against the glass, once, twice, then fell still.

“You left,” he said.

The words were quiet, not accusatory. He said them the way someone might say *the door was open* or *it rained that day*.

She felt a heat rise behind her sternum. “I know.”

“I came home from my shift, and the apartment next door was empty. The mail in the box had ‘Return to Sender’ written on it. The landlady said you’d paid through the end of the month, but you didn’t leave a forwarding address.”

She remembered the landlady. Mrs. Russo, who smelled like mothballs and insisted on calling her Elena. She remembered the hallway carpet, the color of dried blood. She remembered standing in front of Eli’s door at four in the morning, her hand raised, her knuckles two inches from the wood. She hadn’t knocked. She had turned, walked down three flights of stairs, and gotten into a cab that smelled of pine air freshener and regret.

“I’m sorry,” she said now. It came out flat, like she’d ironed it.

He looked at her. The lantern made his eyes black, but she knew they were brown. She’d seen them in morning light, across a shared kitchen table. She’d seen them closed, his head on her shoulder, on the night before his father died.

“I’m not looking for an apology,” he said. “I don’t know what I’m looking for. I’m just here to wash my socks.”

A laugh escaped her, short and surprised. “Sorry about the socks.”

“They’ll survive.” He dug into the pocket of his jacket and pulled out a phone. The screen was dark. “I got a voicemail from you once. About a year after you left.”

She stiffened. “I never left a voicemail.”

“You did. Sometime after midnight. I was working the dinner shift, so I didn’t see it until morning. The message was thirty seconds of static, and then your voice saying ‘Eli’—just my name, like you were testing it—and then a click.” He held the phone up, a dark rectangle. “I’ve still got it. I saved it to the phone when I upgraded. I thought maybe I’d listen to it and figure out what you wanted. But I never did. I couldn’t.”

She pressed her palm against the cool metal of the washing machine. The towels were still in there, sloshing through their final minutes of motion, though the water had stopped churning. She imagined them, linen and cotton, pressing against each other in the dark.

“I don’t remember making that call,” she said.

“That’s okay.”

“I don’t even know if I would have known what to say.”

“It’s okay,” he said again. “I’m not—I don’t need a reason. I just wanted you to know I heard it. Even if I never listened to it.”

She thought about that. About her voice, unremembered, preserved somewhere in the silicon of his life. About the weight of a thing said and unsaid at the same time.

The light flickered, steadied. Somewhere in the back, a circuit breaker hummed. The emergency lights along the baseboard glowed amber for a moment, then went out again.

“I was scared,” she said. It slid out of her, a confession unplanned. “Not of you. Of the part of me that needed you.”

He didn’t flinch. He didn’t reach for her. He just sat, hands resting on his thighs, looking at her in the thin, white light.

“I know,” he said. “I think that’s what I heard. In the static. The scared part.”

She turned back to the machine. Through the glass, she could see the vague shape of the towels, waterlogged and still.

“They’ll be cold when I pull them out,” she said. “I’ll have to take them home and redo the whole load.”

“I could wait with you,” he said. “Till the power comes back. If you want.”

“What about your socks?”

“They’ve waited seven years for a wash. They can wait another hour.”

She didn’t say yes. She didn’t say no. She leaned against the machine, arms crossed, and watched the towel-shaped darkness behind the glass.

He sat on the bench, his hands still still.

The lantern burned on.

Outside, the tide was coming in. Inside, the quiet held.

She didn’t open the door of machine seven. Not yet.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# The Four-Layer Cake of API Governance: Designing Rate Limits for Multi-Tenant Systems

## 1. Authentication, Authorization, Quotas, and Rate Limits – The Critical Distinction

Before designing any rate limiting scheme, you must internalize the difference between four concepts that engineers frequently conflate:

- **Authentication** answers *"Who are you?"* – verifying identity via API keys, OAuth tokens, or mTLS certificates.
- **Authorization** answers *"What are you allowed to do?"* – checking permissions, scopes, and roles after identity is established.
- **Quotas** answer *"How much can you use over a long period?"* – a hard ceiling on total usage (e.g., 10,000 requests per month) enforced at billing boundaries. Quotas are soft limits until exceeded; then they become hard blocks.
- **Rate Limits** answer *"How fast can you use it right now?"* – a per-second or per-minute cap to protect infrastructure from traffic spikes, regardless of remaining quota.

The critical insight: **rate limits protect reliability; quotas protect revenue.** A tenant with 99% of their monthly quota remaining can still be rate-limited to 100 requests per second. Conversely, a tenant with zero quota should be rejected even if they are below the rate limit.

## 2. Token Bucket vs. Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window – Practical Tradeoffs

### Fixed Window
Simplest to implement: count requests in discrete clock-aligned windows (e.g., every minute: 00:00–00:59, 01:00–01:59).  
**Problem:** Traffic bursts at window boundaries. If a tenant sends 100 requests at 00:59 and another 100 at 01:00, the actual spike is 200 requests in two seconds, but each window sees only 100. This punishes legitimate neighbors during "edge collisions."

### Sliding Window Log
Tracks timestamps of every request. When a new request arrives, it removes timestamps older than the window and checks the count.  
**Pros:** Smooth, precise.  
**Cons:** Memory-intensive for high-traffic tenants; O(n) cleanup per request. Not practical at enterprise scale without a sliding window counter approach.

### Sliding Window Counter
Compromises between fixed and log: keeps a counter for the current clock window and a fractional weight for the overlap with the previous window.  
**Tradeoff:** ~5% drift from perfect accuracy, but O(1) operations. Acceptable for most API use cases.

### Token Bucket
The most burst-friendly algorithm. A bucket holds N tokens, refilling at R tokens/second. Each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, the request is denied.  
**Why it wins for multi-tenant APIs:**  
- Natural burst absorption (idle tenants accumulate tokens)  
- No edge collisions  
- Easy to implement via Redis with `SET` + `TTL` for per-tenant buckets  
**Cost:** Requires careful refill rate choice—too slow and small teams fail; too fast and bursts overwhelm the backend.

**Recommendation:** Use Token Bucket for throughput-sensitive endpoints (e.g., write operations) and Sliding Window Counter for read-heavy, low-latency endpoints where burst tolerance is lower priority.

## 3. Don’t Punish the Whole Tenant for One Noisy Integration

Enterprise tenants often have dozens of integrations—some well-behaved, some not. Blocking the entire tenant when one integration floods is a design failure.

**Solution: Two-Level Rate Limiting**

1. **Tenant-level limit:** Total requests per second across all API keys belonging to that tenant. This protects infrastructure from aggregate load.  
2. **Integration-level limit (or API key-level limit):** One tenth (or one fifth) of the tenant limit per individual API key.  

When integration A spikes:  
- Integration A hits its per-key limit and receives 429s.  
- Integration B (well-behaved) continues unaffected under the tenant-level ceiling.  
- If integration A’s volume is small relative to the tenant limit, it may only breach its own key limit without ever touching the tenant ceiling.  

**Critical design rule:** The sum of all per-key limits should exceed the tenant limit. This prevents a situation where every key can independently hit its cap and collectively exceed infrastructure capacity. For example: if tenant limit is 1000 req/s, set per-key limit to 400 req/s. Even with three noisy keys (1200 req/s), the tenant-level guardrail will block the excess.

## 4. Headers and Error Messages – The Engineering Contract

Clients must be told *why* they were limited and *when* they can retry. Return the following headers on every response:

```
X-RateLimit-Limit: 1000              # Tenant-level limit per window
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 235           # Remaining requests in this window
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1704067200        # Unix timestamp when limit resets
Retry-After: 30                      # Seconds to wait (for 429 responses)
```

For 429 errors, the body should return a structured JSON object:

```json
{
  "error": "rate_limit_exceeded",
  "message": "Request rate exceeds the allowed limit for your tenant. Please reduce your request rate or contact support to increase your limit.",
  "retry_after_seconds": 30,
  "limits_applied": [
    {
      "scope": "tenant",
      "name": "requests_per_second",
      "limit": 1000,
      "remaining": 0,
      "reset_at": 1704067200
    }
  ]
}
```

**Differentiate from authorization errors:**  
- 401 Unauthorized → bad authentication  
- 403 Forbidden → valid auth but insufficient permissions  
- 429 Too Many Requests → valid auth, valid permissions, but too fast  

Never return 429 for authentication or authorization failures—that leaks information about valid API keys and confuses clients.

## 5. Observability – Proving the System Works Without Blocking Legitimate Use

Rate limiting becomes a black hole of developer frustration if you cannot answer: *"Was the 429 legitimate, or did your stupid algorithm break my deployment?"*

### Metrics (for dashboards)
- **`rate_limit.requests_total`** by tenant tier, API key, endpoint, and HTTP method  
- **`rate_limit.requests_blocked_total`** by reason (tenant limit, per-key limit)  
- **`rate_limit.current_utilization`** – histogram of how close tenants are to their limit (95th, 99th percentile)  
- **`rate_limit.bucket_fullness`** – for token bucket, current token count as a fraction of capacity  
- **`rate_limit.retry_after_distribution`** – to detect clients aggressively retrying after 30 seconds  

### Logs (for debugging)
Every 429 must produce a structured log with:  
- tenant_id, api_key_id, endpoint, timestamp, retry_after, limit_type  
- request IP (for spotting distributed attacks from a single tenant)  
- current tenant-level and per-key utilization at the moment of rejection  

### Alerts (for operational sanity)
- **Pager (high urgency):** `rate_limit.requests_blocked_total` for the entire system increases >200% in 5 minutes (possible DDoS or batching failure)  
- **Dashboard (medium):** Any single tenant hits their limit more than 10 times in an hour (possible misconfigured client, flag for support outreach)  
- **Report (weekly):** Top 10 blocked requests by tenant – filter out known bad actors, investigate unknowns  

### The “False Positive” Signal
Track `rate_limit.server_error_429` – requests that were blocked but, when replayed manually, succeed. If this exceeds 0.1% of all 429s, your rate limit algorithm has a bug (e.g., clock skew, stale counters, inconsistent bucket refill rates).

## 6. Recommended Baseline Design

For a multi-tenant API supporting teams of 5 to enterprises with thousands of employees, start here:

- **Algorithm:** Token Bucket with a 5-second refill window (burst capacity = 1.5x steady-state limit)  
- **Limits:**  
  - Small teams: 100 req/s tenant, 40 req/s per API key  
  - Mid-tier: 500 req/s tenant, 150 req/s per API key  
  - Enterprise: 2000 req/s tenant, 600 req/s per API key, custom config negotiable  
- **Implementation:** In-memory with Redis backend for distributed workers; TTL on bucket keys (e.g., 60 seconds idle eviction)  
- **Headers:** `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`, `Retry-After`  
- **Fail-open behavior:** If Redis is unreachable, allow requests (set a circuit breaker: 5 seconds of failures → permit all until Redis recovers)  
- **Observability:** Prometheus metrics + structured JSON logging + weekly false-positive review  

This design balances fairness (per-key isolation), predictability (token bucket burst absorption), and debuggability (clear headers and logs). Adjust the refill rate and bursting ratio based on your backend’s actual latency at P99 CPU—measure, don’t guess.

messages.content:
请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters should never have been written in the first place. And of those that are written, a great many are so incomplete or poorly organized that a transaction which could have been settled in two letters drags on through six or eight. Good letters come from clear thinking and careful planning. A sales letter may take weeks to polish; a routine business reply may be written in a few minutes. The point is not how long it takes, but whether the final letter does its job accurately.

There are many rules for judging business letters, but only two are truly essential. First, the letter must make your meaning clear to the reader. Second, it must build goodwill for the company. The best letters do both in the shortest, most courteous, and most complete way. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it—even if it is only a thank-you to a customer. Too much effort is wasted on aimless advertising, ineffective sales pitches, and unnecessary correspondence—words that use up typewriter ribbon and paper but move nothing forward.

A good letter should not draw attention to its own style; it should let the reader receive the message. A publishing house once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Many recipients praised the letters for being well-written—but no one ordered the book. That shows that when words steal the spotlight from the message, even elegant writing fails as business communication. Stationery, letterhead, layout, and paragraphs should all be clean, restrained, and clear. Overly elaborate design or gimmicky formatting only distracts.

Business writing should also respect the reader’s time. Clear subject lines, appropriate salutations, paragraphs organized by topic, a specific request, and complete contact information build trust far more effectively than ornate language. If a letter requires a reply, the writer should state when a response is expected, what information is needed, and what the next step will be. Done this way, the letter does more than convey an attitude—it moves the matter forward.

请将下面的繁体中文寓言/哲理片段翻译成英文，目标读者为青少年读者。要求：保留想象力和节奏；遇到典故不要长篇解释；译文应清楚、流畅，适合作为儿童文学或寓言文本阅读。

素材来源：《莊子的故事》节选：逍遥游开篇
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23913
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
**The Story of the Great Bird (from *Zhuangzi's Stories*)**  

In the Northern Darkness, there was a fish named Kun. Kun was so huge—who knew how many thousand miles long? It changed into a bird, named Peng. Peng’s back—who knew how many thousand miles wide? When it rose in anger and flew, its wings hung like clouds across the sky.  

This bird, when the sea stirred, would journey to the Southern Darkness. The Southern Darkness is the Heavenly Pool.  

*The Qixie* was a book of marvels. It said: “When Peng journeys to the Southern Darkness, it thrashes the water for three thousand miles, then spirals upward on a whirlwind for ninety thousand miles, and rests only after six months.”  

Wild horses (mist), dust—all living things blow breath at one another. The sky’s deep blue—is that its true color? Or is it because it’s so far away, with no end in sight? When Peng looks down from above, it must seem the same.  

If water is not deep enough, it cannot carry a big boat. Spill a cup of water on a hollow floor—a mustard seed can float like a boat. Set a cup there, and it sticks. The water is too shallow, the boat too big.  

If wind is not thick enough, it cannot lift great wings. That is why, at ninety thousand miles, the wind is beneath Peng—then it can ride the wind. With the blue sky on its back and nothing to block it, at last it can plan its journey south.  

A cicada and a little dove laughed at Peng, saying: “We dash up and fly, stop at an elm or a sandalwood tree—sometimes we don’t make it, and just fall to the ground. Why go ninety thousand miles south?”  

If you go to the nearby woods, you bring three meals and come back full. If you go a hundred miles, you grind grain the night before. If you go a thousand miles, you gather food for three months. Those two little creatures—what could they know?  

Small knowledge cannot match great knowledge. Short life cannot match long life. How do we know this? The morning mushroom knows nothing of dawn and dusk. The mayfly knows nothing of spring and autumn. These are short lives.  

In the south of Chu, there was a huge tree called Mingling. It counted five hundred years as spring, and five hundred as autumn. In ancient times, there was a great tree named Dachun. It counted eight thousand years as spring, and eight thousand as autumn. These are long lives.  

And yet Pengzu is famous for his long life. People try to match him—isn’t that sad?  

Even King Tang asked Ji about this. In the barren north, there was a dark sea—the Heavenly Pool. A fish lived there, several thousand miles wide—no one knew how long. Its name was Kun. There was also a bird named Peng, with a back like Mount Tai and wings like clouds across the sky. It spiraled upward on a whirlwind for ninety thousand miles, cutting through clouds, carrying the blue sky, then headed south—straight for the Southern Darkness.  

A quail laughed at it, saying: “Where does he think he’s going? I leap and fly, no more than ten yards and then drop down, flitting among the weeds. That’s the best kind of flying! And where does he think he’s going?”  

This is the difference between small and great.  

So those whose wisdom fits one office, whose conduct suits one village, whose virtue pleases one ruler, who win trust in one kingdom—they see themselves much the same.  

And yet Song Rongzi laughed at them. Even if the whole world praised him, he wouldn’t push harder. If the whole world blamed him, he wouldn’t be upset. He knew the difference between inner and outer, and saw clearly where honor and disgrace began. That was all. He didn’t fuss over worldly things. Still, even he had something he hadn’t yet achieved.  

Liezi rode the wind and drifted along, light and wonderful. After fifteen days, he came back. He didn’t fuss about seeking good fortune. But even though he didn’t walk, he still depended on something.  

But someone who rides the true way of heaven and earth, who rides the changing breath of the six seasons, wandering through endless space—what does *that* one depend on?  

That is why it is said:  
The Perfect Person has no self.  
The Spirit Person has no achievement.  
The Sage has no name.
